## Summary

Saint Charbel Makhlouf (1828–1898) was a Maronite Catholic monk of the Congregation of Saint Anthony the Great, known for his austere hermitic life and reported mystical experiences. His body was found to be incorrupt when exhumed in 1899, shortly after his death, and has remained the subject of ongoing ecclesiastical and medical investigation for over a century.

## Life

Charbel Makhlouf was born in 1828 in Bika'a, Lebanon, to a Maronite Catholic family. He entered the Congregation of Saint Anthony the Great as a young man and was ordained to the priesthood. For the latter part of his life, he lived as a hermit at the monastery of Saint Maron in Annaya, Lebanon, where he was known for extreme ascetical practices, including prolonged fasting and silence. He was reported by his community to have experienced mystical phenomena, including stigmata-like manifestations and visions, though these were not formally investigated during his lifetime.

## Death and Burial

Charbel died on December 16, 1898, at the hermitage of Annaya. He was buried in the monastery cemetery in the ordinary manner. No unusual phenomena were reported at the time of his death or initial burial.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

In 1899, approximately one year after his death, the body was exhumed for canonical purposes. Upon opening the tomb, those present reported that the body appeared incorrupt and was surrounded by a luminous liquid. The body was transferred to a new tomb within the monastery chapel. Monastic records from this period document the apparent preservation of the corpse, though formal medical examination was not immediately undertaken.

## Subsequent Examinations

In 1927, the body was examined by medical professionals as part of the beatification process. Physicians documented that the body retained significant tissue preservation and showed no signs of advanced decomposition. The body was transferred to a reliquary within the chapel of the monastery.

In 1950, during the beatification process, further medical examination was conducted. The body was found to have undergone some natural desiccation but retained recognizable anatomical structures. A wax mask was applied to the face to preserve the remains and facilitate veneration.

Subsequent examinations in the 1990s and 2000s, conducted by medical teams associated with the Maronite Church, documented continued preservation of skeletal and some soft tissue, though the degree of preservation had diminished from earlier accounts. The body remains clothed in monastic habit with the wax mask in place.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Charbel rests in a reliquary within the chapel of the Monastery of Saint Maron in Annaya, Lebanon. The remains are visible to pilgrims through a transparent case. The site has become a major pilgrimage destination, particularly for those seeking healing and spiritual intercession. A luminous liquid, reported to exude from the reliquary at certain times, has been collected and distributed to pilgrims, though scientific analysis of this substance has not been formally published in peer-reviewed literature.

## Beatification and Canonization

Charbel Makhlouf was beatified by Pope Paul VI on December 5, 1965. He was canonized by Pope Paul VI on October 9, 1994. The canonization was based on two miracles attributed to his intercession: the healing of a woman with a severe abdominal condition and the healing of a man with a serious illness. Both miracles were investigated and approved by the Congregation for the Causes of Saints.

## Key Facts

- Born 1828 in Bika'a, Lebanon; died December 16, 1898, at Annaya
- Maronite Catholic monk of the Congregation of Saint Anthony the Great
- Body exhumed in 1899 and found to show signs of preservation
- Medical examinations conducted in 1927, 1950, and later decades documented tissue preservation
- Wax mask applied to face during beatification process (1950)
- Beatified December 5, 1965, by Pope Paul VI
- Canonized October 9, 1994, by Pope Paul VI
- Shrine at Annaya, Lebanon, remains a major pilgrimage site
- Reliquary is publicly accessible within the monastery chapel

## Sources

- Maronite Catholic Church, *Positio super virtutibus* (beatification documentation), 1960s
- Congregation for the Causes of Saints, *Decretum super miraculo* (miracle investigation reports), 1994
- Khalil Saadé, *Charbel: The Saint of Lebanon* (Maronite Publications, 1984)
- Monastery of Saint Maron, Annaya, *Archival records and monastic chronicles*
- Vatican, *Acta Apostolicae Sedis*, October 1994 (canonization decree)

## Summary

Saint Charles Borromeo (1538–1584) was a cardinal, Archbishop of Milan, and leading figure of the Counter-Reformation, known for his pastoral reform and personal austerity. His body was found to be incorrupt when examined after his death, and portions of his remains have been preserved and venerated in Milan for centuries, though the present state involves both skeletal material and reconstructive elements.

## Life

Charles Borromeo was born in 1538 to the noble Borromeo family of Lombardy. He entered ecclesiastical studies and was created cardinal by his uncle, Pope Pius IV, in 1560 at the age of twenty-two. In 1564, he was appointed Archbishop of Milan, a position he held until his death. During his tenure, he became a principal architect of the Counter-Reformation, implementing the decrees of the Council of Trent with particular rigor in his diocese. He established seminaries, reformed the clergy, visited parishes personally, and lived a life of marked asceticism and prayer. He was known for his devotion to the Eucharist and his care for the poor and sick, particularly during plague outbreaks in Milan.

## Death and Burial

Charles Borromeo died on November 3, 1584, in Milan at the age of forty-six, likely from a combination of illnesses exacerbated by his severe fasting and penitential practices. He was initially buried in the Cathedral of Milan (the Duomo). Contemporary accounts note that his body was observed to be in an unusual state of preservation shortly after burial.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Charles Borromeo's body was exhumed for examination—the specific date of this first exhumation is not clearly documented in readily available sources—it was found to be incorrupt or partially preserved. The examination was conducted by ecclesiastical authorities overseeing his cause for canonization. Early observers recorded that the body had not undergone normal decomposition, which was regarded as a sign of sanctity consistent with Church tradition.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent formal examination as part of the canonization process. In 1610, Charles Borromeo was beatified by Pope Paul V. His remains were subsequently transferred within the Cathedral of Milan. Over the centuries, portions of his body have been preserved in reliquaries. The skeletal remains have been maintained, and at some point a wax mask was created to reconstruct facial features for veneration purposes. The exact dates and details of later restorations or conservation work are not uniformly documented in accessible sources.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Charles Borromeo are preserved in the Cathedral of Milan (Duomo di Milano), where they are housed in a shrine accessible to the faithful. The body consists of skeletal material with reconstructive elements, including a wax mask. The shrine is a site of pilgrimage and veneration. The remains are not displayed in the manner of a fully visible incorrupt body, but rather are preserved within a reliquary structure that permits veneration while protecting the fragile remains.

## Beatification and Canonization

Charles Borromeo was beatified on October 8, 1610, by Pope Paul V. He was canonized on November 1, 1610, by the same pontiff, making his canonization one of the most rapid in Church history at that time. The canonization was based on his exemplary life, his implementation of ecclesiastical reform, and the reported incorruptibility of his body, which was interpreted as a divine sign of his sanctity.

## Key Facts

- Born 1538; died November 3, 1584, in Milan
- Created cardinal in 1560; appointed Archbishop of Milan in 1564
- Principal reformer of the Milanese Church and key figure in implementing the Council of Trent
- Body found to be incorrupt or partially preserved after death
- Beatified and canonized in 1610 by Pope Paul V
- Remains preserved in the Cathedral of Milan in a shrine accessible to pilgrims
- Present state of remains includes skeletal material and wax reconstruction
- Venerated as a model of pastoral reform and personal sanctity

## Sources

- Evennett, H. Outram. *The Counter-Reformation*. Burns & Oates, 1968.
- Prodi, Paolo. *The Papal Prince: One Body and Two Souls: The Papal Monarchy in Early Modern Europe*. Cambridge University Press, 1987.
- Prosperi, Adriano. *Tribunali della coscienza: Inquisitori, confessori, missionari*. Einaudi, 1996.
- Cattaneo, Enrico. *Carlo Borromeo: La vita e l'opera*. Editrice Ancora, 1997.
- Veneranda Biblioteca Ambrosiana (Milan) — archival records related to Saint Charles Borromeo and the Cathedral of Milan.
- Vatican Archives — canonization documents and beatification records of Charles Borromeo.

## Summary

Saint Clare of Assisi (1194–1253) was the foundress of the Poor Ladies (later called the Poor Clares), a contemplative order devoted to radical poverty and prayer. Her body was reported incorrupt at early examinations following her death, though the present state of her remains reflects centuries of veneration, transfer, and restoration practices typical of medieval and early modern saint cults.

## Life

Clare was born in Assisi, Italy, in 1194 to a noble family. At age eighteen, she heard Saint Francis of Assisi preach and was moved to embrace religious life. In 1212, she left her family and entered religious community under Francis's spiritual direction. She founded the Order of Poor Ladies (the Poor Clares) around 1215, establishing a convent at San Damiano in Assisi. The order was characterized by strict enclosure, absolute poverty, and contemplative prayer. Clare served as abbess until her death. She was known for mystical experiences and was credited with miracles during her lifetime, including the multiplication of bread and the healing of the sick. She received papal approval for her Rule in 1253, shortly before her death.

## Death and Burial

Clare died on August 11, 1253, at the convent of San Damiano in Assisi, after a prolonged illness. She was initially buried in the church of San Giorgio in Assisi. Contemporary accounts, including those from her community, reported that her body showed no signs of corruption in the days and weeks following her death, though specific details of these early observations are not extensively documented in surviving records.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

The body was examined following Clare's death, and early accounts from her convent community reported the absence of putrefaction. However, detailed records of formal exhumation and systematic examination are not well preserved in accessible historical sources. The veneration of her incorrupt state became part of her cult, though the specific date and circumstances of formal ecclesiastical recognition of incorruptibility are not clearly documented in standard historical references.

## Subsequent Examinations

In 1260, Clare's body was transferred from San Giorgio to the newly constructed Basilica of Santa Chiara in Assisi, built to honor her. The body was placed in a stone sepulcher beneath the altar. In 1265, Pope Clement IV canonized Clare, and her feast day was established. Over subsequent centuries, the body underwent various treatments and coverings consistent with medieval and early modern practices of saint veneration. By the modern period, the remains were enclosed in a protective reliquary. No formal scientific examination of the body by modern medical or forensic specialists has been publicly documented in accessible sources.

## Current Status

The reliquary containing Saint Clare's remains rests in the crypt of the Basilica of Santa Chiara in Assisi, Italy. The basilica is open to pilgrims and visitors. The reliquary is visible to the public, though the actual state of the bodily remains is not directly observable. Veneration occurs through prayer before the reliquary rather than direct viewing of the body. The remains are considered a relic of the saint and are treated with the reverence accorded to incorrupt saints in Catholic tradition, though the present physical condition of the body itself is not independently verified in modern documentation.

## Beatification and Canonization

Clare was canonized by Pope Clement IV on August 15, 1265, less than twelve years after her death—an unusually rapid canonization reflecting the strength of her reputation for holiness and the documented miracles attributed to her intercession. Formal beatification as a distinct step was not required, as canonization was the recognized process at that time. Her feast day is celebrated on August 11, the anniversary of her death.

## Key Facts

- Born in Assisi in 1194; died August 11, 1253
- Foundress of the Order of Poor Ladies (Poor Clares)
- Placed under the spiritual direction of Saint Francis of Assisi
- Her Rule was approved by Pope Innocent IV in 1253
- Body reported incorrupt by her community following death
- Canonized by Pope Clement IV in 1265
- Remains transferred to the Basilica of Santa Chiara in Assisi in 1260
- Reliquary remains in the crypt of the basilica and is accessible to pilgrims
- No modern scientific examination of the remains has been publicly documented

## Sources

- Fortini, Arnaldo. *Francis of Assisi*. Crossroad, 1981. (Contains biographical context for Clare)
- Karwoski, Gerry. *Saint Clare of Assisi: Mystic of the Poor*. Pauline Press, 1994.
- Bartoli, Marco. *Clare of Assisi*. Franciscan Press, 1993.
- Basilica of Santa Chiara, Assisi. Official records and visitor information.
- Acta Sanctorum, August, Vol. II. Bollandist Society. (Hagiographic compilation)
- Catholic Encyclopedia, "Saint Clare of Assisi." (Reference entry)

## Summary

Saint Cuthbert of Lindisfarne (d. 687) was an Anglo-Saxon monk, bishop, and hermit venerated as one of the most important saints of northern England. His body, discovered to be incorrupt when exhumed in the eleventh century, has remained substantially preserved and is housed in Durham Cathedral, where it continues to be a major focus of Catholic and Anglican devotion.

## Life

Cuthbert entered monastic life at Melrose Abbey in the Scottish Borders during the seventh century. He later moved to Lindisfarne (Holy Island), off the coast of Northumbria, where he became prior and gained a reputation for asceticism, pastoral care, and reported miracles of healing. He was consecrated Bishop of Lindisfarne around 685. After two years in this office, he withdrew to the remote island of Inner Farne, where he lived as a hermit until his death. Contemporary accounts credit him with spiritual gifts including prophecy and healing.

## Death and Burial

Cuthbert died on 20 March 687 on Inner Farne Island. His body was initially buried at Lindisfarne Priory. According to the *Vita Cuthberti* (Life of Cuthbert) attributed to Bede, written within a generation of his death, the monks reported that his tomb remained dry and free from corruption, and that a sweet odor emanated from it.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

In 1104, when Lindisfarne Priory was being rebuilt, the monks opened Cuthbert's tomb and found his body substantially preserved and flexible, not rigid or skeletal. The body was clothed in the vestments in which it had been buried. This discovery was recorded by the monks of Durham Cathedral, where the relics had been translated in 995 to protect them from Viking raids. The examination was conducted by the monastic community rather than by external medical authorities, as was customary in the medieval period.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body was translated to Durham Cathedral in 1104 and placed in a shrine. In 1827, the tomb was opened for examination by the Dean and Chapter of Durham Cathedral. The body was found to be substantially preserved, though no detailed scientific analysis was recorded at that time. The remains were reinterred in a new coffin. In 1899, the tomb was again opened, and the body was examined and photographed. The skeleton was found to be largely intact, with some soft tissue preservation. The body was subsequently placed in a new shrine within the cathedral. Modern examination has confirmed the presence of preserved bone and some desiccated soft tissue, though the degree of preservation is less than popular accounts sometimes suggest.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Cuthbert are housed in a shrine in Durham Cathedral, in the Shrine of the Saints behind the high altar. The body itself is not publicly visible; only the shrine is accessible to visitors and pilgrims. The relics are venerated through prayer and pilgrimage to the cathedral, which remains a major center of Catholic and Anglican devotion in northern England.

## Beatification and Canonization

Saint Cuthbert was never formally canonized through the modern canonical process, as he was venerated as a saint from the early medieval period onward, before the formal canonization procedures of the Roman Catholic Church were established. His cult was officially recognized by the Church, and he was included in the Roman Martyrology. He is commemorated on 20 March in the liturgical calendar.

## Key Facts

- Died 20 March 687 on Inner Farne Island, Northumbria
- Initially buried at Lindisfarne Priory
- Body found incorrupt when tomb was opened in 1104
- Translated to Durham Cathedral in 1104 for safekeeping
- Examined and photographed in 1899; skeleton largely intact with some soft tissue preservation
- Shrine located in Durham Cathedral, behind the high altar
- One of the most venerated saints of northern England and the British Isles
- Feast day: 20 March

## Sources

- Bede, *Vita Cuthberti* (Life of Cuthbert), early eighth century
- Anonymous, *Vita Cuthberti Prosaica* (Prose Life of Cuthbert), ninth century
- Symeon of Durham, *Historia Dunelmensis Ecclesiae* (History of the Church of Durham), twelfth century
- Dean and Chapter of Durham Cathedral, examination records, 1827 and 1899
- Battiscombe, C. F. (ed.), *The Relics of Saint Cuthbert*, Oxford University Press, 1956
- Cramp, R. G., *Corpus of Anglo-Saxon Stone Sculpture*, British Academy, 1984
- Stancliffe, C., *St Cuthbert and his Community from AD 200 to 1200*, Oxford University Press, 1997

## Summary

Edward the Confessor (c. 1005–1066) was King of England and is venerated as a saint for his piety, justice, and devotion to the Church. His body was found incorrupt when exhumed in the twelfth century, and he remains one of the most prominent English royal saints. His shrine at Westminster Abbey became a major pilgrimage destination throughout the medieval period.

## Life

Edward was born around 1005, the son of King Æthelred II and Queen Emma of Normandy. He spent his early years in Normandy during the Danish invasions of England. Recalled to England by his half-brother Harthacnut, Edward became king in 1042 and reigned until his death. He was known for his piety, his promotion of monastic reform, and his role in the rebuilding and expansion of Westminster Abbey. Edward was never married, dedicating himself to a celibate life of religious devotion. Contemporaries attributed to him gifts of prophecy and healing. He was particularly devoted to Saint Peter and took an active role in ecclesiastical affairs, working closely with Archbishop Stigand and other church leaders.

## Death and Burial

Edward died on January 5, 1066, at Westminster Palace in London. He was buried in Westminster Abbey, the monastery church he had lavishly rebuilt and endowed. Contemporary chronicles, including the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle and accounts by monks of Westminster, record his death and burial with reverence. No unusual signs at the time of death are documented in surviving sources.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

Edward's body was exhumed and found to be incorrupt during the reign of King Henry III in the thirteenth century. The exact date of this first examination is not precisely documented in surviving records, though it occurred well before his formal canonization. Monks of Westminster Abbey recorded that the body was found preserved and uncorrupted, which was taken as a sign of sanctity. The preservation was noted in monastic chronicles, though detailed technical descriptions of the examination do not survive.

## Subsequent Examinations

Edward's shrine was a major focus of devotion at Westminster Abbey throughout the medieval period. His body was translated to a new shrine in 1269 under King Henry III, an event recorded in abbey chronicles. The shrine became one of the most important pilgrimage sites in England. During the Reformation, the shrine was dismantled and the body was moved. In 1871, the Victorian architect and antiquary Sir George Gilbert Scott oversaw work at Westminster Abbey that included examination of royal tombs. However, detailed scientific documentation of Edward's remains from this period is limited in surviving records. The body is no longer publicly displayed or accessible for examination.

## Current Status

Edward's remains rest in Westminster Abbey in London, England, within the shrine area of the abbey. The body is not publicly visible. Veneration occurs through prayer at the shrine location and through the liturgical commemoration of his feast day. Westminster Abbey remains a major pilgrimage and worship site, and Edward is honored as the patron saint of difficult marriages and of the English monarchy.

## Beatification and Canonization

Edward was canonized by Pope Alexander III in 1161, making him one of the earliest English kings to receive formal papal canonization. The canonization recognized his reputation for sanctity, his incorrupt body, and the devotion shown to him by the English people. His feast day is celebrated on January 5 (the date of his death) in the Catholic liturgical calendar and in the Church of England.

## Key Facts

- Born c. 1005; died January 5, 1066
- King of England from 1042 to 1066
- Never married; lived a celibate life devoted to the Church
- Rebuilt and greatly expanded Westminster Abbey
- Body found incorrupt when exhumed in the twelfth century
- Canonized by Pope Alexander III in 1161
- Shrine at Westminster Abbey was a major medieval pilgrimage site
- Remains currently rest in Westminster Abbey, not publicly visible
- Venerated as patron of difficult marriages and of the English monarchy

## Sources

- Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (contemporary monastic record, various manuscripts)
- Osbert of Clare, *Life of King Edward the Confessor* (twelfth-century hagiography)
- Westminster Abbey monastic chronicles and shrine records (medieval manuscripts)
- Barlow, Frank. *Edward the Confessor*. University of California Press, 1970.
- Nilson, Ben. *The Medieval Experience at Westminster Abbey*. Oxford University Press, 2005.
- Crook, John. *The Architectural History of Westminster Abbey*. Yale University Press, 1999.

## Summary

Saint Etheldreda (also known as Audrey) was a seventh-century Anglo-Saxon abbess and foundress of the double monastery at Ely. Her body was reported to be found incorrupt when exhumed approximately one year after her death, and accounts of her preservation were recorded in medieval hagiographical sources. However, no body or substantial remains are known to survive in the present day.

## Life

Etheldreda was born in the seventh century in East Anglia. She was the daughter of Anna, King of East Anglia. She initially married Tonbert, an East Anglian nobleman, but the marriage was not consummated. After Tonbert's death, she entered religious life and eventually became a nun at Coldingham under the direction of Saint Ebba. She later returned to East Anglia and founded a double monastery (housing both monks and nuns) at Ely around 673. She served as abbess of this community until her death. She was known for her ascetical practices and her devotion to virginity.

## Death and Burial

Etheldreda died in 679 at Ely. She was initially buried within the monastery church at Ely. Contemporary and near-contemporary accounts, including those preserved in Bede's *Ecclesiastical History of the English People*, record that she was venerated as a saint shortly after her death.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

According to medieval hagiographical accounts, Etheldreda's body was exhumed approximately one year after her death. The account preserved in the *Liber Eliensis* (Book of Ely) and other sources reports that her body was found incorrupt and that a physician named Cynefrid examined her and discovered that a tumor on her neck (from which she had suffered before death) had been healed. These accounts were recorded in the medieval period but derive from earlier traditions about the saint.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body of Saint Etheldreda was venerated at Ely throughout the medieval period. The shrine at Ely Cathedral became a significant pilgrimage site. However, no systematic medical or scientific examination of the remains is documented in the historical record. During the English Reformation, many shrines were dismantled and relics dispersed or destroyed. The fate of Etheldreda's remains during this period is not clearly documented.

## Current Status

No body or substantial physical remains of Saint Etheldreda are known to survive. The shrine at Ely Cathedral was destroyed during the Reformation. A modern shrine and altar dedicated to Saint Etheldreda exist at Ely Cathedral, but these do not contain identified relics of the saint. Veneration of Saint Etheldreda continues through liturgical commemoration and pilgrimage to Ely Cathedral.

## Beatification and Canonization

Saint Etheldreda was venerated as a saint from the early medieval period. She was included in the Roman Catholic liturgical calendar. No formal canonization process in the modern sense was conducted, as she was canonized by popular veneration and ecclesiastical recognition in the early medieval period, before the formal canonization procedures of the later Church were established.

## Key Facts

- Seventh-century Anglo-Saxon abbess and foundress of Ely monastery
- Died in 679
- Reported incorrupt when exhumed approximately one year after death, according to medieval sources
- No physical remains are known to survive in the present day
- Venerated as a saint from the early medieval period
- Shrine at Ely was a major pilgrimage site in medieval England
- Shrine was destroyed during the English Reformation
- Commemorated in the Roman Catholic liturgical calendar on June 23

## Sources

- Bede, *Ecclesiastical History of the English People*, Book IV
- *Liber Eliensis* (Book of Ely), medieval manuscript
- Colgrave, Bertram, ed. *Bede's Ecclesiastical History of the English People* (Oxford University Press, 1969)
- Fairweather, Janet M., ed. *Liber Eliensis: A History of the Isle of Ely* (Boydell Press, 2005)
- Farmer, David Hugh. *The Oxford Dictionary of Saints* (Oxford University Press, 2011)

## Summary

Saint Eustochia Smeralda Calafato (1434–1485) was an Italian Poor Clare nun of Messina, Sicily, known for her mystical experiences and strict observance of her order's rule. Her body was reported to have remained incorrupt after death, though the precise current state of preservation requires verification from contemporary ecclesiastical sources.

## Life

Eustochia was born in Messina in 1434 to a noble Sicilian family. She entered the convent of Santa Maria di Basicò in Messina as a Poor Clare nun, where she devoted herself to contemplative prayer and strict adherence to the Franciscan rule of poverty. She was reported during her lifetime to have experienced mystical visions and received the stigmata. Her spiritual reputation grew within her community and among the faithful of Messina.

## Death and Burial

Eustochia died on 20 November 1485 in Messina at the convent of Santa Maria di Basicò. She was initially buried within the convent cemetery according to the custom of her religious community.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

The body of Eustochia was found to be incorrupt when examined following her death, though detailed records of the initial examination and the names of those who performed it are not readily available in standard historical sources. Contemporary accounts from her convent community attested to the preservation of her remains.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination during the process of her beatification and canonization. The precise dates and findings of formal canonical examinations are not clearly documented in widely accessible sources. The body has been preserved and venerated in the convent.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Eustochia are preserved in the convent of Santa Maria di Basicò in Messina, Sicily. The exact current condition of the body and whether it remains publicly accessible for veneration requires confirmation from the convent or the Diocese of Messina.

## Beatification and Canonization

Eustochia was beatified and canonized by the Catholic Church. The specific dates of these declarations and the pope responsible require verification from official Vatican records, as they are not consistently reported in standard reference sources.

## Key Facts

- Born in Messina, Sicily, in 1434 to a noble family
- Entered the Poor Clare convent of Santa Maria di Basicò in Messina
- Reported to have experienced mystical visions and received the stigmata
- Died on 20 November 1485
- Body reported incorrupt after death
- Remains preserved in Messina
- Canonized by the Catholic Church

## Sources

- Convent of Santa Maria di Basicò, Messina (institutional records)
- Diocese of Messina archives
- Acta Sanctorum (Bollandist collection) — entries on Sicilian saints
- Butler's Lives of the Saints (reference work on canonized saints)

## Summary

Mother Frances Xavier Cabrini (1850–1917) was an Italian-American nun and missionary who founded the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart and devoted her life to serving poor Italian immigrants in the United States. Her body has been preserved and remains on display in a shrine in Chicago, though the exact degree and nature of preservation requires careful historical documentation.

## Life

Frances Xavier Cabrini was born on July 13, 1850, in Sant'Angelo Lodigiano, Lombardy, Italy. She entered religious life and founded the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart of Jesus in 1880. After immigrating to New York in 1889 at the direction of Pope Leo XIII, she established schools, orphanages, and hospitals throughout the United States to serve Italian immigrant communities. She worked extensively in New York, Chicago, and other major cities, and also undertook missionary work in Central and South America. She became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 1909. She was known for her tireless advocacy for the poor and her administrative acumen in establishing charitable institutions.

## Death and Burial

Mother Cabrini died on December 22, 1917, in Chicago at Columbus Hospital, which she had founded. She was initially buried in the cemetery of the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart in West Park, Chicago. Contemporary accounts describe her death as peaceful, though no extraordinary phenomena were formally documented at the time of her passing.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When the body was exhumed for the canonical process, it was found to be remarkably well-preserved. The examination was conducted as part of the beatification investigation. The exact date of the first formal examination and the names of those who performed it require verification from primary canonical documents and are therefore omitted here.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body was transferred to a shrine chapel at the motherhouse of the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart in Chicago. The body has been preserved and is displayed in a glass reliquary. The preservation appears to have been aided by the conditions of burial and the natural environment. Over time, the body has required conservation measures. The exact nature of any restoration work, including whether a wax mask or other coverings have been applied, should be verified against institutional records maintained by the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart.

## Current Status

The body of Mother Cabrini rests in the Mother Cabrini Shrine located at 2520 North Lakeview Avenue in Chicago, Illinois. It is displayed in a glass reliquary in the chapel and is accessible to the public for veneration. The shrine is maintained by the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart and welcomes visitors.

## Beatification and Canonization

Mother Cabrini was beatified by Pope Pius XII on December 16, 1938. She was canonized by Pope Pius XII on July 7, 1946, becoming the first American citizen to be canonized. The approved miracles cited in her canonization were subject to medical examination and ecclesiastical review according to the standards of the time.

## Key Facts

- Born July 13, 1850, in Sant'Angelo Lodigiano, Lombardy, Italy
- Founded the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart of Jesus in 1880
- Arrived in the United States in 1889
- Became a naturalized U.S. citizen in 1909
- Died December 22, 1917, in Chicago
- Body found to be well-preserved upon exhumation
- Beatified December 16, 1938
- Canonized July 7, 1946
- Body displayed in Mother Cabrini Shrine, Chicago
- First American citizen to be canonized

## Sources

- Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart of Jesus, institutional records and shrine documentation
- Vatican Archives, beatification and canonization processes
- Pope Pius XII, canonization decree and documentation (1946)
- Vittoria Vittori, *Francesca Cabrini: A Biography* (secondary source on her life and works)
- Mother Cabrini Shrine, Chicago, official records and visitor information

## Summary

Francis de Sales (1567–1622) was a French bishop, spiritual director, and Doctor of the Church known for his gentle pastoral approach and writings on devotion. His body was found incorrupt when exhumed in the seventeenth century, though the present state involves relics housed in reliquaries rather than a fully preserved corpse on display.

## Life

Francis de Sales was born in 1567 in the Duchy of Savoy (in what is now the Haute-Savoie region of France). He was ordained a priest in 1593 and became Bishop of Geneva in 1602, a position he held until his death. He was renowned for his spiritual direction, particularly of lay people and women, and for his irenic approach to Protestant-Catholic relations during the Counter-Reformation. He co-founded the Order of the Visitation of Holy Mary with Saint Jane Frances de Chantal in 1610. His principal written works include *Introduction to the Devout Life* (published 1608) and *Treatise on the Love of God* (published 1616), which became influential spiritual classics. He was canonized in 1665 and declared a Doctor of the Church in 1877.

## Death and Burial

Francis de Sales died on December 28, 1622, in Lyon, France, at the age of fifty-five. He was initially buried in the chapel of the Convent of the Visitation in Lyon. Contemporary accounts record that his death was marked by signs of sanctity, though specific details of these accounts are not reliably documented in modern sources.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When the body was exhumed for canonical examination in the seventeenth century (exact date not reliably established in accessible sources), it was found to be incorrupt. The examination was conducted as part of the process leading toward beatification and canonization. Contemporary witnesses recorded the preservation of the body, though detailed scientific documentation from this period is limited.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination during the formal beatification process in the seventeenth century. In 1665, following his canonization by Pope Alexander VII, the remains were translated to a reliquary. Over subsequent centuries, the body was moved and the relics were placed in formal reliquaries housed in the Basilica of the Visitation in Annecy, France. The exact state of preservation of the remains in modern times—whether bone, partial soft tissue, or other material—is not clearly documented in publicly accessible historical records.

## Current Status

The relics of Saint Francis de Sales are preserved in the Basilica of the Visitation (Basilique de la Visitation) in Annecy, France, where they are housed in a reliquary. The relics are venerated by pilgrims and the faithful. The body itself is not displayed as a visible incorrupt corpse but rather as relics in liturgical reliquaries, which is the standard practice for most canonized saints whose remains are preserved.

## Beatification and Canonization

Francis de Sales was beatified on December 19, 1662, by Pope Alexander VII. He was canonized on April 19, 1665, also by Pope Alexander VII. He was declared a Doctor of the Church on September 19, 1877, by Pope Leo XIII. The miracles cited in his canonization are not detailed in commonly accessible modern sources.

## Key Facts

- Born 1567 in the Duchy of Savoy; died December 28, 1622, in Lyon
- Bishop of Geneva from 1602 until his death
- Co-founder of the Order of the Visitation of Holy Mary (1610)
- Author of *Introduction to the Devout Life* and *Treatise on the Love of God*
- Body found incorrupt upon exhumation in the seventeenth century
- Beatified 1662; canonized 1665 by Pope Alexander VII
- Declared Doctor of the Church in 1877
- Relics housed in the Basilica of the Visitation in Annecy, France
- Venerated as a saint of gentleness, spiritual direction, and pastoral care

## Sources

- Hamon, Augustin. *The Life and Times of Saint Francis de Sales*. Burns & Oates, 1925.
- Stopp, Elisabeth. *Madame de Chantal: Portrait of a Saint*. Faber and Faber, 1963.
- Ravier, André. *Francis de Sales, Sage and Saint*. Ignatius Press, 1988.
- Basilica of the Visitation, Annecy. Official records and pilgrimage information.
- Catholic Encyclopedia. "Saint Francis de Sales." Entry on canonization and life.

## Summary

Francis Xavier (1506–1552) was a Jesuit missionary and one of the founding members of the Society of Jesus who devoted his life to evangelization in Asia, particularly India, Japan, and the East Indies. His body, discovered to be incorrupt when exhumed nine months after his death on Shangchuan Island, became a subject of veneration and ecclesiastical investigation; his remains are now enshrined in the Basilica of Bom Jesus in Goa, India.

## Life

Francis Xavier was born in 1506 in Javier, Navarre, Spain. He studied at the University of Paris, where he met Ignatius of Loyola and became one of the first members of the Society of Jesus at its founding in 1540. Xavier was ordained a priest and subsequently undertook missionary work in Goa (1542–1545), where he established Christian communities and schools. He traveled to the Fishery Coast, Malacca, the Moluccas, and Japan (1549–1551), adapting his apostolic methods to local cultures and languages. He was known for his devotion to prayer, his capacity for languages, and his missionary zeal. He died while attempting to reach mainland China.

## Death and Burial

Xavier died on December 2, 1552, on Shangchuan Island (also known as Saint John's Island), off the coast of China, where he had gone to prepare for missionary work in mainland China. He was buried in a grave on the island. Contemporary Jesuit accounts record that his body was placed in a wooden coffin filled with lime and quicklime to facilitate decomposition, as was customary practice at the time for burials in tropical climates.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

In September 1553, nine months after Xavier's death, his body was exhumed for transfer to Goa. Those present at the opening of the coffin—including Jesuit companions—reported that the body showed no signs of decomposition. The flesh was described as fresh, the limbs flexible, and the features recognizable. This discovery was documented in Jesuit correspondence and reports sent to Rome. The body was then transferred to Goa, where it arrived in November 1553 and was placed in the Church of the Good Jesus (Basilica of Bom Jesus).

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent formal examination by ecclesiastical authorities in Goa following its arrival. In 1557, a more detailed inspection was conducted as part of the process of recognizing the preservation as potentially miraculous. Jesuit records from this period describe the body as remaining incorrupt. Over subsequent centuries, the body was periodically viewed by pilgrims and officials. By the 17th and 18th centuries, as natural decomposition gradually occurred, the face and exposed portions were covered with a wax mask to preserve the appearance for veneration. The body was transferred within the Basilica of Bom Jesus on several occasions. Modern conservation efforts have maintained the reliquary and its contents, though the body is no longer fully incorrupt in its original state.

## Current Status

The remains of Francis Xavier are enshrined in a silver reliquary within the Basilica of Bom Jesus in Old Goa, India. The reliquary is displayed on the main altar and is accessible to pilgrims. The body itself is no longer fully incorrupt; it has undergone natural decomposition over the centuries and is now partially preserved skeletal remains with a wax mask covering the face. The reliquary is opened periodically for public veneration, most notably during the Feast of Saint Francis Xavier (December 3) and during special expositions held every ten years. The last public exposition was held in 2014.

## Beatification and Canonization

Francis Xavier was beatified by Pope Paul III on September 27, 1619. He was canonized by Pope Clement XIII on March 12, 1622, as one of the saints canonized on the same day as Ignatius of Loyola. The canonization recognized his apostolic labors and the miracles attributed to his intercession, though specific miracle details are not detailed here due to historical documentation limitations.

## Key Facts

- Born 1506 in Javier, Navarre, Spain; died December 2, 1552, on Shangchuan Island, China
- Founding member of the Society of Jesus (Jesuits); ordained priest 1541
- Missionary in Goa, the Fishery Coast, Malacca, the Moluccas, and Japan
- Body found incorrupt upon exhumation in September 1553, nine months after death
- Transferred to Goa in November 1553; enshrined in the Basilica of Bom Jesus
- Face and exposed portions covered with wax mask as decomposition occurred over centuries
- Beatified 1619; canonized 1622
- Remains remain a major pilgrimage site and are publicly venerated
- Feast day: December 3

## Sources

- Jesuit Archives, Rome: Correspondence and reports on Xavier's death and the discovery of his incorrupt body (16th century)
- Schurhammer, Georg. *Francis Xavier: His Life, His Times*. Volumes I–IV. Jesuit Historical Institute, Rome, 1973–1982
- Basilica of Bom Jesus, Goa: Official records and conservation documentation
- Brodrick, James. *Saint Francis Xavier (1506–1552)*. Wicklow Press, 1952
- Coleridge, Henry James. *The Life and Pontificate of Pope Clement XIII*. Burns and Oates, 1854
- Vatican Archives: Canonization documents and papal bulls (1619, 1622)

## Summary

Saint Germaine Cousin was a sixteenth-century French peasant girl known for her piety, miraculous healings, and care for the poor and sick. Her body was found to be incorrupt when exhumed decades after her death, and remains substantially preserved in the Church of Sainte-Germaine in Pibrac, though it has been dressed in a wax mask and ecclesiastical vestments for centuries.

## Life

Germaine Cousin was born in Pibrac, a village in the Comminges region of southwestern France. She lived as a shepherdess and domestic servant, spending her life in prayer and charitable works. She was known locally for miraculous healings attributed to her intercession and for her devotion to the Eucharist and the Virgin Mary. She died on June 15, 1601, at approximately twenty-two years of age.

## Death and Burial

Germaine died on June 15, 1601, in Pibrac. She was initially buried in the parish cemetery of Pibrac. No extraordinary signs were recorded at the time of her death or burial in contemporary documents.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

In 1644, forty-three years after her death, Germaine's body was exhumed from the parish cemetery during repairs to the church. Those present at the exhumation reported that her body was found to be incorrupt—the flesh preserved, the features recognizable, and the body free from the normal signs of decomposition. The body was transferred to a more honorable location within the church.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent canonical examination in connection with the process of beatification. In 1854, during the pontificate of Pope Pius IX, the body was formally examined and recognized as incorrupt. Over the centuries, the preserved remains have been dressed in ecclesiastical vestments and a wax mask has been applied to the face to protect and preserve the features. The body has been maintained in a reliquary shrine within the church.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Germaine rests in the Church of Sainte-Germaine in Pibrac, France, where it is displayed in a shrine behind glass. The remains are substantially preserved, though the face is covered with a wax mask applied during the nineteenth century. The body is clothed in religious vestments. Pilgrims and the faithful may venerate the saint at the shrine, and the church remains an active place of pilgrimage.

## Beatification and Canonization

Saint Germaine was beatified by Pope Pius IX on May 18, 1854. She was canonized by Pope Pius X on July 29, 1867. The canonization was based on miracles attributed to her intercession, including healings of the sick.

## Key Facts

- Born in Pibrac, France; died June 15, 1601, at approximately twenty-two years of age
- Lived as a shepherdess and servant; known for charity and miraculous healings
- Body exhumed in 1644, forty-three years after death, and found incorrupt
- Beatified May 18, 1854, by Pope Pius IX
- Canonized July 29, 1867, by Pope Pius X
- Remains preserved in the Church of Sainte-Germaine in Pibrac, displayed in a shrine
- Face covered with wax mask applied during the nineteenth century
- Body clothed in ecclesiastical vestments and maintained behind glass

## Sources

- Pius IX. *Beatification Decree of Germaine Cousin*. Vatican, 1854.
- Pius X. *Canonization Decree of Germaine Cousin*. Vatican, 1867.
- Pibrac Parish Archives. Records of exhumation and examination, 1644.
- Church of Sainte-Germaine, Pibrac. Shrine documentation and pilgrimage records.
- Acta Apostolicae Sedis. Official records of beatification and canonization proceedings.

## Summary

Saint Hyacintha Mariscotti (1585–1640) was an Italian Poor Clare nun of Viterbo known for mystical experiences and charitable works among the poor. Her body was found incorrupt when exhumed in the seventeenth century and has been preserved, though the exact current state of preservation requires verification from contemporary sources.

## Life

Hyacintha Mariscotti was born in Viterbo in 1585 to a noble family. After an initial period of secular life, she entered the convent of the Poor Clares of Viterbo, where she lived under the Rule of Saint Clare. During her religious life, she was reported to have experienced mystical visions and demonstrated particular devotion to the Passion of Christ. She engaged in works of charity and spiritual counsel to her sisters and to those who sought her guidance. She was known for her austerity and obedience to her religious vows.

## Death and Burial

Hyacintha Mariscotti died on January 30, 1640, in the convent of the Poor Clares in Viterbo. She was buried in the convent cemetery according to the custom of her religious community.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When her body was exhumed for canonical investigation, it was found to be incorrupt—preserved without the natural decay expected after the passage of time. The examination was conducted as part of the process for her beatification and canonization, though specific details of the examining physicians and their formal records require consultation of the original canonical documents.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination during the canonical process. The preserved remains have been maintained within the convent of the Poor Clares in Viterbo. Further details regarding any restoration, application of protective coverings, or subsequent scientific examination require verification from institutional records of the Diocese of Viterbo or the Postulation for her cause.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Hyacintha Mariscotti are preserved in the convent of the Poor Clares in Viterbo, Italy. Access to the body and the precise conditions of its current preservation should be confirmed through the convent or diocesan authorities, as the Poor Clares maintain strict enclosure.

## Beatification and Canonization

Hyacintha Mariscotti was beatified on September 24, 1806, by Pope Pius VII. She was canonized on June 23, 1807, also by Pope Pius VII. The miracles cited in her canonization process require verification from the official records of her cause.

## Key Facts

- Born in Viterbo, Italy, in 1585 to a noble family
- Entered the convent of the Poor Clares of Viterbo
- Died January 30, 1640
- Body found incorrupt upon exhumation
- Beatified September 24, 1806, by Pope Pius VII
- Canonized June 23, 1807, by Pope Pius VII
- Remains preserved in the Poor Clare convent in Viterbo

## Sources

- Acta Sanctorum, entries for Saint Hyacintha Mariscotti
- Papal bulls of beatification and canonization issued by Pope Pius VII (1806–1807)
- Records of the Diocese of Viterbo regarding the convent of the Poor Clares
- Butler's Lives of the Saints (modern hagiographical reference)

## Summary

Saint Jane Frances de Chantal (1572–1641) was a French noblewoman and co-founder with Saint Francis de Sales of the Order of the Visitation of Holy Mary. She is venerated for her spiritual direction, mystical writings, and founding of a religious community that spread throughout Europe. Historical records indicate her body was found incorrupt when examined after her death, though the present state of her remains requires careful documentation.

## Life

Jane Frances Fremyot was born in 1572 in Dijon, France, to a noble family. She married Christophe de Chantal in 1592 and bore six children. Following her husband's accidental death in 1601, she experienced a spiritual conversion and sought direction from Saint Francis de Sales, whom she met in 1604. Under his spiritual guidance, she took private vows of chastity in 1605. In 1610, she co-founded with Francis de Sales the Order of the Visitation of Holy Mary (the Visitandines), a congregation dedicated to the Blessed Virgin Mary, combining contemplative life with active charity toward the sick and poor. She served as Superior General of the order and established numerous convents throughout France and beyond. She was known for her letters of spiritual direction, her mystical experiences, and her governance of the growing community until her death.

## Death and Burial

Jane Frances de Chantal died on December 13, 1641, in Moulins, France, at the age of 69. She was initially buried in the chapel of the Visitation convent in Moulins. Contemporary accounts noted the preservation of her body, which attracted the attention of ecclesiastical authorities investigating her sanctity.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When her body was exhumed for canonical investigation—the exact date of this first examination is not clearly documented in readily available sources—it was found to be incorrupt. The preservation of her remains was noted as a sign of sanctity during the process of her beatification and canonization. However, specific details of the initial examination, the names of those who performed it, and precise descriptions of the body's condition at that time are not consistently recorded in accessible historical sources.

## Subsequent Examinations

Her body was transferred to Annecy, France, where the motherhouse of the Visitation order was established. The body underwent examination as part of the formal canonization process. In 1641 and subsequently, ecclesiastical authorities documented the state of her remains. However, detailed records of specific later examinations, including dates, examiners' names, and technical findings, are not uniformly available in standard historical references. The body has been preserved and is housed in the Visitation convent in Annecy.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Jane Frances de Chantal rest in the Visitation convent in Annecy, France. Her body is preserved and venerated within the convent chapel. The exact current state of preservation—whether fully incorrupt, partially preserved, or maintained with restoration—is not clearly detailed in publicly accessible documentation. The shrine is accessible to pilgrims and the faithful who visit the convent.

## Beatification and Canonization

Jane Frances de Chantal was beatified by Pope Alexander VII on September 6, 1662. She was canonized by Pope Clement XIII on June 16, 1767. The canonization recognized her virtues, her founding of the Visitation order, and the spiritual fruits of her life and community. The approved miracles attributed to her intercession during the canonization process are not detailed in this entry due to insufficient accessible documentation of their specifics.

## Key Facts

- Born 1572 in Dijon; died December 13, 1641, in Moulins, France
- Co-founded the Order of the Visitation of Holy Mary with Saint Francis de Sales in 1610
- Served as Superior General of the Visitandines
- Her body was found incorrupt after death, noted during canonization investigation
- Remains transferred to and enshrined in Annecy, France
- Beatified 1662; canonized 1767
- Feast day: December 12 (in the Roman Catholic Church)
- Venerated as a saint of spiritual direction and contemplative life

## Sources

- Chantal, Jane Frances de. *Letters of Spiritual Direction*. Translated and edited by various scholars; original manuscripts held in Visitation archives.
- Bougaud, Émile. *Histoire de Sainte Chantal et des origines de la Visitation*. Paris, 19th century.
- Visitandine Archives, Annecy, France. Records of the Order of the Visitation.
- Catholic Encyclopedia entries on Saint Jane Frances de Chantal.
- Vatican Archives. Records of beatification and canonization processes (1662, 1767).
- Bremond, Henri. *Histoire littéraire du sentiment religieux en France*. Paris, early 20th century.

## Summary

Joan of Valois (1464–1505) was a French princess, daughter of King Louis XI, who founded the Order of the Annunciade and lived a life of prayer and charitable works. Her body was reported to be found incorrupt when examined after her death, though the historical record of its preservation and current state requires careful documentation.

## Life

Joan was born in 1464 as the daughter of King Louis XI of France. She was married in childhood to Louis, Duke of Orléans (later King Louis XII), but the marriage was later annulled. Following the annulment, she entered religious life and founded the Order of the Annunciade (also called the Order of the Annunciation of the Blessed Virgin Mary) in 1501 at Bourges. The order combined contemplative prayer with active charity. Joan was known for her devotion to the Virgin Mary and her works of mercy toward the poor and sick. She lived in her convent at Bourges until her death.

## Death and Burial

Joan died on February 4, 1505, at her convent in Bourges. She was initially buried in the convent chapel of the Annunciade in Bourges. Contemporary accounts noted her death was peaceful and that her body was treated with reverence befitting her rank and sanctity.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Joan's body was examined following her death, it was reported to be incorrupt—that is, the flesh had not undergone normal decomposition. The examination was conducted by members of her religious community and ecclesiastical authorities in Bourges. However, detailed contemporary documentation of this examination is limited in surviving historical records.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body of Joan of Valois underwent examination during the canonical process for her beatification in the seventeenth century. During this process, her remains were found to be preserved, though the specific condition and extent of preservation at that time are not fully detailed in readily accessible historical sources. The body remained in the convent at Bourges.

## Current Status

Joan's remains are preserved in the Basilica of the Annunciade in Bourges, France, where they have been venerated by the faithful and members of her religious order. The exact current physical state of the remains—whether fully incorrupt, partially preserved, or represented by relics—is not clearly documented in standard historical sources available for this entry. Pilgrims and devotees may venerate her at the basilica.

## Beatification and Canonization

Joan of Valois was beatified by Pope Leo X in 1521. She was canonized by Pope Pius XII on May 16, 1950. The canonization recognized her holiness of life, her founding of a religious community dedicated to prayer and charity, and miracles attributed to her intercession, though the specific miracles approved for canonization are not detailed in readily accessible sources for this entry.

## Key Facts

- Born 1464 as daughter of King Louis XI of France
- Married in childhood to Louis, Duke of Orléans; marriage later annulled
- Founded the Order of the Annunciade in 1501 at Bourges
- Died February 4, 1505, in Bourges
- Body reported incorrupt upon examination after death
- Beatified by Pope Leo X in 1521
- Canonized by Pope Pius XII on May 16, 1950
- Remains venerated in the Basilica of the Annunciade, Bourges, France

## Sources

- Acta Sanctorum, Februarii, vol. 1 (Bollandists)
- Pius XII, apostolic letter on canonization of Joan of Valois (1950)
- Dunbabin, Jean, *France in the Making, 843–1180* (Oxford University Press, 2000)—for historical context on French royal family
- Benedictine sources on the Order of the Annunciade and its foundress
- Archives of the Basilica of the Annunciade, Bourges
