## Summary

Saint Joaquina de Vedruna (1783–1854) was a Spanish foundress who established the Congregation of the Sisters of Mary Immaculate and devoted her life to education and care for the poor. Her body was reported to be found incorrupt when examined during the canonization process, though the precise current state of preservation requires verification from ecclesiastical records.

## Life

Joaquina de Vedruna was born in 1783 in Vic, Catalonia. She married and bore nine children before being widowed. Following her husband's death, she entered religious life and founded the Congregation of the Sisters of Mary Immaculate (Carmelitas de Caridad) in 1826, dedicated to teaching poor children and caring for the sick and elderly. She was known for her spiritual direction, administrative acumen, and commitment to serving marginalized populations in Catalonia. She lived in Barcelona during her later years and directed her congregation until her death.

## Death and Burial

Joaquina de Vedruna died on August 28, 1854, in Barcelona. She was initially buried in the convent of her congregation in Barcelona.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

During the canonical investigation for her beatification, her body was exhumed and examined. Contemporary accounts from the beatification process recorded that her body was found in a state of preservation that was deemed unusual and worthy of documentation, though specific details of the examination and the examiners' names and findings require confirmation from the official diocesan records.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination as part of the formal canonization process conducted by the Catholic Church. Further details regarding subsequent transfers, restoration, or the application of preservative measures require verification from the Congregation for the Causes of Saints.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Joaquina de Vedruna are preserved in the convent of the Sisters of Mary Immaculate in Barcelona, Spain. The precise accessibility for public veneration and the current physical condition of the body should be confirmed directly with the congregation or the Archdiocese of Barcelona.

## Beatification and Canonization

Joaquina de Vedruna was beatified by Pope Pius XII on May 22, 1940. She was canonized by Pope John Paul II on March 18, 1984. The approved miracles cited in her canonization process require verification from the official decree of canonization published by the Vatican.

## Key Facts

- Born in Vic, Catalonia, in 1783; died in Barcelona on August 28, 1854
- Widow who founded the Congregation of the Sisters of Mary Immaculate in 1826
- Dedicated to education of poor children and care for the sick and elderly
- Body reported preserved during canonical examination
- Beatified May 22, 1940 (Pope Pius XII)
- Canonized March 18, 1984 (Pope John Paul II)
- Feast day: August 28

## Sources

- Congregation for the Causes of Saints, official decree of canonization (March 18, 1984)
- Archdiocese of Barcelona, diocesan records of beatification process
- Carmelitas de Caridad (Sisters of Mary Immaculate), congregational archives
- Pius XII, beatification decree (May 22, 1940)

## Summary

Saint John Bosco (1815–1888), known as Don Bosco, was an Italian Catholic priest and educator who founded the Salesian Society and dedicated his life to the spiritual and practical education of poor and abandoned boys. His body was found to be incorrupt when first examined after death, though the precise degree and duration of preservation remains documented primarily through early ecclesiastical records rather than modern scientific analysis.

## Life

John Bosco was born on August 16, 1815, in Castelnuovo d'Asti, Piedmont. He entered seminary studies and was ordained a priest in 1841. He devoted himself to pastoral work among the poor youth of Turin, establishing the Oratory of Saint Francis de Sales in 1847, which became the foundation of his educational mission. In 1859, he founded the Society of Saint Francis de Sales (Salesians), a religious congregation dedicated to education and missionary work. Bosco was known for his pedagogical innovations, his spiritual direction of young people, and his reported supernatural gifts, including alleged visions and bilocation. He established schools, printing presses, and trade workshops to provide practical formation alongside religious instruction.

## Death and Burial

John Bosco died on January 31, 1888, in Turin at the age of 72. He was initially buried in the Church of Mary Help of Christians (Chiesa di Maria Ausiliatrice) in Turin, the principal church of his Salesian community. Contemporary accounts from his community recorded that his death was marked by peaceful circumstances befitting his sanctity, though no formal medical documentation of unusual signs at death survives in widely accessible records.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

The body of Don Bosco was exhumed for canonical examination on September 6, 1929, during the process leading toward his beatification. Ecclesiastical examiners reported that the body showed signs of preservation inconsistent with the natural decay expected after more than forty years of burial. The examination was conducted under the supervision of the Salesian community and Church authorities in Turin, though detailed scientific protocols and independent medical verification records from this examination are not extensively documented in modern published sources.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body was examined again in connection with the advancement of his canonization cause. In 1934, prior to his beatification, further inspection was undertaken. The body was transferred to a place of honor within the Church of Mary Help of Christians. Over the decades, the remains have been preserved in a reliquary or shrine within the church. Modern scientific examination using contemporary forensic or radiological methods does not appear to have been formally conducted or published in peer-reviewed literature, limiting precise documentation of the current state of preservation.

In 2009, in preparation for the bicentennial of Don Bosco's birth (1815–2015), the Salesian community at Turin opened the saint's reliquary, re-vested the body, and re-encased it in a new transparent urn for public veneration. From 2010 through 2015 a second reliquary containing the right hand of Don Bosco was sent on an international peregrination, visiting Salesian houses, schools, and parishes in more than 130 countries before returning to Turin for the bicentennial celebrations. The body itself remained at the Basilica of Mary Help of Christians throughout.
## Current Status

The remains of Saint John Bosco rest in the Church of Mary Help of Christians in Turin, Italy, where they are housed in a shrine accessible to the faithful. The body is not displayed in an open casket but is preserved within a reliquary. Pilgrims and devotees may venerate the saint at this location. The exact condition of the physical remains—whether fully incorrupt, partially preserved, or represented by skeletal remains with reconstructive coverings—is not detailed in widely available contemporary sources, and the Church has not issued recent public documentation of the body's present state.

## Beatification and Canonization

John Bosco was beatified by Pope Pius XI on June 2, 1934. He was canonized by Pope Pius XI on April 1, 1935. The canonization recognized his extraordinary holiness, his foundational work in Catholic education, and the spiritual fruits of his apostolate. The approved miracles cited in the canonization process are documented in the official records of the Salesian order and the Vatican, though the specific details of these miracles are not enumerated here without access to the complete canonical documentation.

## Key Facts

- Born August 16, 1815; died January 31, 1888, in Turin
- Founded the Salesian Society in 1859
- Established the Oratory of Saint Francis de Sales in 1847
- Body exhumed September 6, 1929, and found to show signs of preservation
- Beatified June 2, 1934, by Pope Pius XI
- Canonized April 1, 1935, by Pope Pius XI
- Remains housed in the Church of Mary Help of Christians, Turin
- Venerated as a saint of education, youth ministry, and spiritual direction
- Modern scientific examination of the remains has not been formally published in accessible academic sources

## Sources

- Salesian Society official records and archives, Turin
- Vatican Congregation for the Causes of Saints, canonization documentation for John Bosco
- Lemoyne, Giovanni Battista. *Biographical Memoirs of Saint John Bosco*. Salesian Publishers.
- Bosco, John. *The Memoirs of Saint John Bosco*. New Bosco Books.
- Pontifical records of Pope Pius XI regarding beatification and canonization, 1934–1935
- Church of Mary Help of Christians (Maria Ausiliatrice), Turin—institutional records and shrine information

## Summary

Saint John Nepomucene was a 14th-century Catholic priest and confessor in Prague who became a martyr under King Wenceslaus IV. His body was reported to have been found incorrupt when exhumed in the 17th century, though the historical record regarding the degree and permanence of this preservation remains contested among scholars.

## Life

John Nepomucene was born in the 14th century in Pomuk (or Nepomuk), Bohemia. He entered the priesthood and became a canon at the Cathedral of Saint Vitus in Prague. He served as a confessor and was known for his devotion to the sacrament of penance. He held the position of vicar general of the Prague archdiocese under Archbishop Jan of Jenstein.

## Death and Burial

John Nepomucene died on March 20, 1393. According to tradition, he was martyred by order of King Wenceslaus IV, allegedly thrown from the Charles Bridge in Prague into the Vltava River. The circumstances of his death—whether by drowning, prior torture, or other means—are not uniformly documented in contemporary sources. His body was recovered and initially buried in the Church of the Holy Cross in the Old Town of Prague.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

In 1621, during the process of transferring his remains, the body was reported to have been found in a state of preservation. Witnesses at the exhumation recorded observations of an intact corpse. However, detailed contemporary documentation of this examination is limited, and accounts vary regarding the degree of preservation observed.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body was transferred to the Cathedral of Saint Vitus in Prague, where it was enshrined. In 1729, during canonization proceedings, the body was examined again. By this time, the remains showed significant deterioration. The body was subsequently placed in a reliquary and later dressed with a wax mask and ecclesiastical vestments to present an intact appearance for veneration. The original skeletal remains are preserved beneath this external covering.

## Current Status

The reliquary containing the remains of Saint John Nepomucene is housed in the Cathedral of Saint Vitus in Prague, in a chapel dedicated to him. The body is not naturally incorrupt; it is presented with a wax mask and vestments. The reliquary is accessible to pilgrims and the faithful for veneration.

## Beatification and Canonization

Saint John Nepomucene was beatified by Pope Clement XII in 1721. He was canonized by Pope Clement XII on March 19, 1729. The canonization was based on miracles attributed to his intercession and his recognition as a martyr and confessor of the faith.

## Key Facts

- Born in Pomuk, Bohemia; died March 20, 1393
- Served as vicar general of the Prague archdiocese
- Died under King Wenceslaus IV; traditionally said to have been thrown from the Charles Bridge
- Body reported incorrupt upon exhumation in 1621, though documentation is sparse
- By 1729, remains had deteriorated significantly
- Presented for veneration with wax mask and vestments over skeletal remains
- Beatified 1721; canonized 1729 by Pope Clement XII
- Reliquary located in the Cathedral of Saint Vitus, Prague
- Venerated as patron saint of confessors and those in danger of drowning

## Sources

- Acta Sanctorum, March, volume 3 (Bollandists)
- Thurston, Herbert. *The Physical Phenomena of Mysticism*. Burns Oates, 1952.
- Vauchez, André. *Sainthood in the Later Middle Ages*. Cambridge University Press, 1997.
- Archdiocese of Prague, Cathedral of Saint Vitus records and reliquary documentation
- Clement XII, papal bull of canonization, 1729

## Summary

Jean-Baptiste-Marie Vianney (1786–1859), known as the Curé of Ars, was a French Catholic priest renowned for his pastoral devotion, spiritual direction, and reported supernatural gifts, particularly his ability to read consciences in the confessional. His body was found to be remarkably preserved when exhumed in the early twentieth century, though it has since undergone restoration and is now displayed in a wax effigy rather than in its original state.

## Life

Jean-Baptiste-Marie Vianney was born in 1786 in Dardilly, France, during the French Revolution. He entered seminary studies relatively late and was ordained a priest in 1815. In 1818, he was assigned to the small rural parish of Ars-sur-Formans in the Ain department, where he remained for over forty years until his death.

During his ministry at Ars, Vianney became known for his austere personal practices, including severe fasting and self-mortification. He spent long hours in the confessional and attracted pilgrims from across France seeking his spiritual counsel. He was credited with converting numerous lapsed Catholics and was reported by contemporaries to possess knowledge of penitents' sins without their verbal confession—a phenomenon attributed to supernatural insight. He was also said to have experienced visions and encounters with demonic temptation.

Vianney's reputation for holiness grew substantially during his lifetime, and he was recognized as an exemplary model of priestly virtue and pastoral care.

## Death and Burial

Jean-Baptiste-Marie Vianney died on August 4, 1859, at the age of seventy-three, after a prolonged illness. He was initially buried in the parish cemetery at Ars-sur-Formans. Contemporary accounts record that his funeral drew large numbers of pilgrims and that devotion to him began immediately after his death.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

In 1904, forty-five years after his death, Vianney's body was exhumed for canonical examination in preparation for beatification. The exhumation revealed that his body had undergone minimal decomposition despite the passage of decades and burial in ordinary soil. Contemporary witnesses and ecclesiastical officials recorded that the remains were substantially preserved, with recognizable facial features and intact skeletal structure.

## Subsequent Examinations

Following the 1904 exhumation, the body was transferred to a reliquary within the church at Ars. Medical and canonical examinations were conducted as part of the beatification process. However, the body continued to deteriorate gradually over the subsequent decades.

In 1925, following Vianney's beatification, the remains underwent further examination and conservation work. By the mid-twentieth century, the original body had deteriorated sufficiently that ecclesiastical authorities commissioned the creation of a wax effigy to preserve the saint's appearance for public veneration. The wax mask was fitted over the preserved skeletal remains and dressed in priestly vestments.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint John Vianney, displayed as a wax effigy over preserved bone, rest in a glass reliquary in the Basilica of Ars-sur-Formans. The effigy is publicly visible and accessible to pilgrims. The basilica remains an active pilgrimage site, and the saint's incorruptibility—understood in its historical context as partial preservation followed by artistic restoration—continues to be venerated as a sign of his sanctity.

## Beatification and Canonization

Jean-Baptiste-Marie Vianney was beatified on January 8, 1925, by Pope Pius XI. He was canonized on May 31, 1929, also by Pope Pius XI. The canonization recognized his exemplary pastoral ministry and the spiritual fruits of his intercession. The approved miracles attributed to his intercession are documented in the canonical processes, though specific details of individual miracles are recorded in the Vatican archives.

## Key Facts

- Born 1786 in Dardilly, France; died August 4, 1859, at Ars-sur-Formans
- Ordained priest in 1815; assigned to Ars in 1818, where he served for over forty years
- Known for extreme personal austerity, lengthy hours in confession, and reported supernatural knowledge of penitents' sins
- Body exhumed in 1904 and found substantially preserved
- Beatified January 8, 1925, by Pope Pius XI
- Canonized May 31, 1929, by Pope Pius XI
- Remains now displayed as a wax effigy over preserved bone in the Basilica of Ars-sur-Formans
- Declared patron saint of parish priests by Pope Pius XI in 1929

## Sources

- Monnin, A. M. *The Life of the Curé of Ars* (English translation, 1860)
- Trochu, François. *The Curé d'Ars: Saint Jean-Baptiste-Marie Vianney* (1927)
- Vatican Archives: Beatification and Canonization Process of Jean-Baptiste-Marie Vianney
- Basilica of Ars-sur-Formans: Official historical records and pilgrimage documentation
- Pius XI. *Rerum Ecclesiae* (encyclical, 1926) and canonization decree (1929)

## Summary

Joseph of Cupertino (1603–1663) was an Italian Conventual Franciscan friar known during his lifetime for reported mystical experiences, including levitation during prayer. His body was found to be incorrupt when exhumed in the 18th century, and he was canonized by Pope Clement XII in 1767.

## Life

Joseph was born in Cupertino, in the Kingdom of Naples, on June 17, 1603. He entered the Conventual Franciscan order and was ordained a priest. During his religious life, he was reported by contemporaries to experience ecstatic states and levitation, particularly during the celebration of Mass and moments of prayer. He served in various friaries and was known for his devotion and mystical experiences. He died at the friary in Osimo.

## Death and Burial

Joseph died on September 18, 1663, at the friary of Osimo in the Papal States. He was initially buried in the friary cemetery. Contemporary accounts record that his death was marked by the gathering of the faithful, though specific details of any unusual signs at death are not well-documented in reliable sources.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Joseph's body was exhumed for canonical examination in the 18th century, it was found to be incorrupt. The exact date of this first exhumation is not clearly established in accessible historical records. The examination confirmed that the body had resisted natural decomposition, which was noted as remarkable by those who conducted the inspection.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent canonical examination as part of the beatification process. In 1753, during the formal beatification proceedings, the body was examined and its incorrupt state was documented. The body was transferred to a shrine within the friary church at Osimo, where it was placed in a reliquary. Over time, the remains have been preserved in a protected shrine setting.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Joseph of Cupertino rests in the friary church at Osimo, Italy, where it is venerated by pilgrims. The remains are housed in a shrine and are accessible for veneration. The exact current condition of the body—whether fully incorrupt, partially preserved, or maintained with restoration—is not clearly documented in widely available sources.

## Beatification and Canonization

Joseph of Cupertino was beatified by Pope Benedict XIV on May 24, 1753. He was canonized by Pope Clement XII on July 16, 1767. The canonization recognized his holiness and the miracles attributed to his intercession, though specific details of the approved miracles are not included here due to insufficient accessible documentation.

## Key Facts

- Born June 17, 1603, in Cupertino, Kingdom of Naples
- Entered the Conventual Franciscan order
- Reported to experience ecstatic prayer and levitation during his lifetime
- Died September 18, 1663, at Osimo
- Body found incorrupt upon exhumation in the 18th century
- Beatified May 24, 1753, by Pope Benedict XIV
- Canonized July 16, 1767, by Pope Clement XII
- Venerated at the friary church in Osimo, Italy

## Sources

- Pastrovicchi, Marcellino. *Vita di San Giuseppe da Cupertino* (biographical work on the saint's life)
- Thurston, Herbert. *The Physical Phenomena of Mysticism* (historical treatment of reported mystical phenomena)
- Acta Sanctorum (Bollandist collection, entries on Saint Joseph of Cupertino)
- Vatican Archives, Beatification and Canonization Records of Pope Benedict XIV and Pope Clement XII

## Summary

Saint Juliana Falconieri (1270–1341) was a Florentine mystic and foundress of the Servite Third Order, known for her devotion to the Eucharist and spiritual direction of her community. Her body was reported to have remained incorrupt after death, though the historical record regarding the present state of her remains requires careful qualification.

## Life

Juliana was born in Florence in 1270 to the noble Falconieri family. She received her religious formation under the direction of Saint Alexis Falconieri, one of the Seven Founders of the Servite Order. Around 1287, she established a community of women living according to the Rule of the Third Order of Servites, dedicating herself to contemplative life, charitable works, and Eucharistic devotion. She was known among her contemporaries for mystical experiences, including reported visions and a particular charism of spiritual counsel. She remained in Florence throughout her life, leading her community with recognized holiness.

## Death and Burial

Juliana died on June 19, 1341, in Florence at approximately seventy years of age. She was buried in the Church of the Annunziata in Florence, the principal church of the Servite Order. Contemporary accounts from her community recorded that her body showed no signs of decomposition in the days following her death, and that a sweet odor was perceived near her tomb.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

The body was examined and found to be incorrupt when the tomb was opened for canonical investigation. The exact date of this formal examination is not clearly documented in readily accessible sources. Witnesses from the Servite community attested to the preservation of her remains, and these testimonies were recorded as part of the process for her canonization.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination during the formal canonization process in the sixteenth century. However, detailed records of specific medical or scientific findings from these examinations are not consistently preserved in modern historical documentation. The body was moved and venerated within the Church of the Annunziata over subsequent centuries.

## Current Status

Saint Juliana's remains are preserved in the Church of the Annunziata in Florence. The body is not currently on public display. Veneration occurs through prayer at her shrine within the church. The present physical condition of the remains—whether fully incorrupt, partially preserved, or maintained through restoration—is not clearly documented in publicly available sources, and caution should be exercised in making definitive claims about the current state of preservation.

## Beatification and Canonization

Juliana was beatified by Pope Clement XII in 1737. She was canonized by Pope Benedict XIII on June 19, 1737 (the feast day of her death). The canonization recognized her sanctity of life, her founding of the Third Order community, and the reported miracles attributed to her intercession, though specific details of the approved miracles are not consistently recorded in standard reference works.

## Key Facts

- Born in Florence, 1270; died June 19, 1341
- Foundress of the Servite Third Order for women
- Known for Eucharistic devotion and mystical prayer
- Body reported incorrupt after death
- Beatified 1737; canonized 1737
- Venerated in the Church of the Annunziata, Florence
- Feast day: June 19

## Sources

- Acta Sanctorum, June, volume 4 (Bollandists)
- Vies des Saints (Lives of the Saints), Servite Order archives
- Chevalier, Cédric. *Histoire de l'Ordre des Servites* (historical overview of Servite Order)
- Church of the Annunziata, Florence (institutional records)
- Catholic Encyclopedia entry on Saint Juliana Falconieri

## Summary

Saint Lucy Filippini (1672–1732) was an Italian educator and foundress of the Maestre Pie, a congregation dedicated to the Christian education of girls. Her body was found to be incorrupt when exhumed, and she was canonized in 1930 by Pope Pius XI.

## Life

Lucy Filippini was born in 1672 in Tarquinia, in the Papal States. She entered religious life and became known for her dedication to educating poor girls, founding schools in collaboration with Cardinal Marcantonio Barbarigo. She established the Maestre Pie (Pious Teachers), a congregation of women religious devoted to Christian education. She was recognized during her lifetime for her spiritual wisdom and pastoral effectiveness in forming young women in faith and virtue.

## Death and Burial

Lucy Filippini died on 9 March 1732 in Montefiascone, Italy, where she had established one of her schools. She was initially buried in the church of the Maestre Pie in Montefiascone.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When her body was exhumed for canonical examination, it was found to be incorrupt—preserved without the normal processes of decomposition. The examination confirmed the unusual preservation of her remains, which contributed to the recognition of her sanctity and supported the advancement of her cause for canonization.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent canonical examination as part of the beatification process. Her remains were preserved and maintained in the convent of the Maestre Pie in Montefiascone. Details of specific medical or scientific examinations conducted during the canonization process are not extensively documented in readily available historical records.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Lucy Filippini rests in the church of the Maestre Pie in Montefiascone, Italy, where it is venerated by the congregation and the faithful. The remains are preserved in a reliquary within the church and are accessible for veneration.

## Beatification and Canonization

Lucy Filippini was beatified on 26 May 1926 by Pope Pius XI. She was canonized on 24 June 1930 by Pope Pius XI. The canonization recognized her virtues and the miracles attributed to her intercession.

## Key Facts

- Born 1672 in Tarquinia, Papal States
- Foundress of the Maestre Pie (Pious Teachers), a congregation for girls' education
- Died 9 March 1732 in Montefiascone, Italy
- Body found incorrupt upon exhumation
- Beatified 26 May 1926 by Pope Pius XI
- Canonized 24 June 1930 by Pope Pius XI
- Feast day: 26 March
- Remains venerated in Montefiascone

## Sources

- Acta Apostolicae Sedis, Vol. 22 (1930) — official record of canonization by Pope Pius XI
- Congregatio de Causis Sanctorum — documentation of beatification and canonization processes
- Maestre Pie congregation archives, Montefiascone — institutional records of the foundress and her remains
- Butler's Lives of the Saints, revised edition — biographical reference

## Summary

Madeleine Sophie Barat (1789–1865) was a French religious educator and founder of the Society of the Sacred Heart, a congregation dedicated to women's education. Her body was reported to be incorrupt when examined after her death, though the historical record regarding its present state of preservation requires careful qualification.

## Life

Madeleine Sophie Barat was born on December 12, 1789, in Joigny, France. She entered religious life and, in 1800, became a member of the Dilette di Gesù (Daughters of Jesus). In 1801, she co-founded the Society of the Sacred Heart (Société du Sacré-Cœur) with Jean Varin, S.J., and Joseph Varin. The society expanded rapidly across Europe and beyond, establishing schools and educational institutions for girls. Barat served as Superior General of the congregation for sixty years. She was known for her spiritual direction, administrative acumen, and commitment to forming young women in faith and learning.

## Death and Burial

Madeleine Sophie Barat died on May 25, 1865, in Paris at the age of seventy-five. She was initially buried in the chapel of the Society of the Sacred Heart's motherhouse in Paris. Contemporary accounts noted that her death was marked by the veneration of the religious community she had founded.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Barat's body was exhumed for canonical examination, it was reported to be in a state of preservation that attracted attention from those investigating her cause for canonization. However, detailed contemporary documentation of the specific findings, the date of exhumation, or the names of those who conducted the examination is not reliably established in accessible historical sources.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body of Madeleine Sophie Barat was transferred to the Society of the Sacred Heart's convent in Jette, Belgium, where it has been preserved. No peer-reviewed medical or scientific examination of her remains has been published in modern scholarly literature. The precise current state of preservation—whether the body remains fully incorrupt, partially preserved, or maintained through restoration techniques—is not documented in sources available to this entry.

## Current Status

The remains of Madeleine Sophie Barat are housed at the convent of the Society of the Sacred Heart in Jette, Belgium. Access to the body for public veneration or scholarly examination is restricted. The exact condition and presentation of the remains are not publicly documented in detail.

## Beatification and Canonization

Madeleine Sophie Barat was beatified on June 24, 1908, by Pope Pius X. She was canonized on May 24, 1925, by Pope Pius XI. The miracles cited in her canonization process are documented in the official records of the Congregation for the Causes of Saints, though specific details of these miracles are not included here without access to the complete canonical documentation.

## Key Facts

- Founded the Society of the Sacred Heart in 1801, which became a major international congregation dedicated to women's education
- Served as Superior General for sixty years
- Died in Paris on May 25, 1865
- Body transferred to Jette, Belgium
- Beatified by Pope Pius X in 1908
- Canonized by Pope Pius XI in 1925
- Reported to be incorrupt upon examination, though modern scientific documentation is lacking

## Sources

- Société du Sacré-Cœur archives (institutional records, Jette, Belgium)
- Congregation for the Causes of Saints, official records of canonization process
- Baunard, Louis. *Vie de la Mère Madeleine Sophie Barat* (19th-century biographical work)
- Catholic Encyclopedia entries on Saint Madeleine Sophie Barat and the Society of the Sacred Heart
- Papal documents: beatification decree (1908) and canonization bull (1925)

## Summary

Saint Margaret Mary Alacoque (1647–1690) was a French Visitation nun whose mystical experiences and devotion to the Sacred Heart profoundly shaped Catholic spirituality. Her body was found incorrupt when exhumed decades after her death, and she was canonized in 1920 by Pope Benedict XV.

## Life

Margaret Mary Alacoque was born on July 31, 1647, in Verosvres, Burgundy, France. She entered the Visitation convent at Paray-le-Monial in 1671 at age twenty-four. During her religious life, she reported receiving private revelations concerning devotion to the Sacred Heart of Jesus, which she communicated to her spiritual director, Claude de la Colombière, a Jesuit priest. These revelations emphasized reparation, the First Friday devotion, and the Holy Hour. She held various positions within the convent, including portress and assistant infirmarian. She was known for her obedience, humility, and mystical experiences, though these were initially viewed with caution by ecclesiastical authorities.

## Death and Burial

Margaret Mary Alacoque died on October 17, 1690, at the Visitation convent in Paray-le-Monial, at age forty-three, after a prolonged illness. She was buried in the convent cemetery. No extraordinary signs at the time of her death were formally documented in the canonical record.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When her body was exhumed on September 25, 1715—twenty-five years after her death—it was found to be remarkably preserved. The exhumation was conducted in the presence of convent officials and witnesses. Contemporary accounts recorded that her body showed no signs of decomposition, her features remained recognizable, and her religious habit was intact. The preservation was noted as unusual given the length of time elapsed and the conditions of burial.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body was examined again during the beatification process in the eighteenth century. In 1864, during the canonization proceedings, the body was transferred to a reliquary within the chapel of the Visitation convent at Paray-le-Monial. Medical and ecclesiastical examiners documented the continued preservation of her remains. Over time, the body was placed in a wax effigy or protective covering to preserve it further and to present it in a reverent manner for veneration. The exact current state of the underlying remains has not been subject to modern scientific analysis published in peer-reviewed literature.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Margaret Mary Alacoque rest in the Visitation convent chapel at Paray-le-Monial, France. Her body, preserved in a reliquary with a wax effigy, is accessible to pilgrims and the faithful who visit the shrine. The convent remains an active pilgrimage site dedicated to devotion to the Sacred Heart.

## Beatification and Canonization

Margaret Mary Alacoque was beatified on September 18, 1864, by Pope Pius IX. She was canonized on May 13, 1920, by Pope Benedict XV. The canonization recognized her role in promoting devotion to the Sacred Heart and the spiritual fruits of her mystical teachings within the Church.

## Key Facts

- Born July 31, 1647, in Verosvres, Burgundy; died October 17, 1690, at Paray-le-Monial
- Entered the Visitation convent in 1671
- Reported private revelations concerning the Sacred Heart of Jesus
- Body exhumed September 25, 1715, and found incorrupt twenty-five years after death
- Beatified September 18, 1864, by Pope Pius IX
- Canonized May 13, 1920, by Pope Benedict XV
- Remains preserved in reliquary at the Visitation convent, Paray-le-Monial
- Her spiritual legacy significantly influenced Catholic devotional practice

## Summary

Margaret of Castello (1287–1320) was a Dominican tertiary and mystic venerated for her spiritual gifts, particularly her devotion to the Eucharist and her care for the poor and sick in Città di Castello. Her body was reported to be found incorrupt when exhumed, though the historical record regarding its present state of preservation requires careful qualification.

## Life

Margaret was born in Metola, near Città di Castello, in the Papal States. She was blind from birth. Despite her blindness, she received religious instruction and entered the Third Order of Saint Dominic as a tertiary. She lived a life of prayer, fasting, and service to the poor and infirm in Città di Castello. She was known for her mystical experiences and her devotion to the Blessed Sacrament. She died at approximately thirty-three years of age.

## Death and Burial

Margaret died in Città di Castello on April 13, 1320. She was buried in the Church of San Domenico in Città di Castello. Contemporary accounts note that her body was regarded with veneration by the local faithful immediately after her death.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Margaret's body was exhumed for canonical investigation, it was reported by witnesses to be incorrupt. The exact date of this first exhumation is not clearly documented in accessible historical sources. Local ecclesiastical authorities recorded the finding, which contributed to the growth of her cult.

## Subsequent Examinations

Margaret's remains have been examined multiple times in connection with her canonization process. The body was transferred within the Church of San Domenico. However, detailed scientific or medical examination records from these investigations are not readily available in published form. The present condition of the remains reflects centuries of preservation within a church environment.

## Current Status

Margaret's body rests in the Church of San Domenico in Città di Castello, Italy, where it is preserved and venerated. The remains are not on permanent public display but are accessible to pilgrims and the faithful through the church. Her tomb is a site of devotion within the local Catholic community.

## Beatification and Canonization

Margaret was beatified by Pope Benedict XIII on June 19, 1609. She was canonized by Pope Francis on September 24, 2023, making her one of the most recently canonized saints. The canonization recognized her holiness of life and the spiritual fruits of her intercession.

## Key Facts

- Born in Metola, near Città di Castello, in 1287; blind from birth
- Entered the Third Order of Saint Dominic as a tertiary
- Died April 13, 1320, in Città di Castello at approximately thirty-three years of age
- Body reported incorrupt upon exhumation for canonical investigation
- Beatified by Pope Benedict XIII in 1609
- Canonized by Pope Francis in 2023
- Venerated particularly for her mystical devotion and care for the poor and sick
- Buried and venerated in the Church of San Domenico, Città di Castello

## Summary

Margaret of Cortona (1247–1297) was an Italian Franciscan tertiary and mystic whose spiritual life was marked by radical penitence and devotion to Christ's Passion. Her body was found to be incorrupt when examined after her death, and she was canonized by Pope Benedict XIII in 1728. The present state of her remains involves a skeletal structure with preserved soft tissue in places, displayed within a protective shrine in Cortona.

## Life

Margaret was born in Montepulciano, Tuscany, in 1247. In her youth she lived as the mistress of a nobleman; after his murder, she experienced a profound spiritual conversion. She entered the Third Order of Saint Francis and settled in Cortona around 1277, where she devoted herself to extreme ascetical practices, including fasting, self-mortification, and contemplative prayer. She was known among her contemporaries for mystical experiences, including reported visions of Christ and the Virgin Mary. Margaret established a hospital and worked among the poor and sick. She was recognized as a spiritual director and counselor during her lifetime. She died on February 22, 1297, in Cortona.

## Death and Burial

Margaret died in Cortona on February 22, 1297, at approximately fifty years of age. She was initially buried in the Church of San Basilio in Cortona. Contemporary accounts record that her body emitted a sweet odor after death, a detail noted in early hagiographical sources, though such reports require cautious interpretation given the conventions of medieval spiritual literature.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Margaret's body was exhumed for canonical investigation—the precise date of this first examination is not clearly documented in accessible sources—her remains were found to show unusual preservation. The body retained soft tissue and was not subject to the expected decomposition. These findings supported her reputation for sanctity and contributed to the advancement of her cause for canonization.

## Subsequent Examinations

Margaret's body underwent examination as part of the formal canonization process in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. The remains were found to be substantially preserved, though not completely incorrupt in all parts. In accordance with practices of the period, the skeletal structure was arranged and displayed within a shrine. The body has been maintained in the Basilica of Santa Margherita d'Antignano in Cortona, where it remains visible to pilgrims within a protective reliquary.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Margaret of Cortona rest in the Basilica of Santa Margherita d'Antignano in Cortona, Italy. Her body is displayed within a shrine and is accessible to the public for veneration. The remains consist of a skeletal framework with areas of preserved soft tissue. The shrine is maintained by the Franciscan community and remains a site of pilgrimage.

## Beatification and Canonization

Margaret of Cortona was beatified by Pope Clement XII on September 18, 1737. She was canonized by Pope Benedict XIII on May 18, 1728. (Note: The chronological sequence of these dates as recorded in some sources requires clarification from primary papal documents.) The canonization was based on her reputation for sanctity, her mystical gifts, and the preservation of her body after death.

## Key Facts

- Born in Montepulciano, Tuscany, in 1247; died in Cortona on February 22, 1297
- Entered the Third Order of Saint Francis and lived a life of radical penitence in Cortona
- Her body was found to be substantially preserved when examined for canonization
- Canonized by Pope Benedict XIII in the early eighteenth century
- Her remains are housed in the Basilica of Santa Margherita d'Antignano in Cortona and are publicly venerable
- She is venerated as a saint of penance, mystical prayer, and service to the poor

## Summary

Saint Maria Bertilla Boscardin (1888–1922) was an Italian Ursuline nun and nurse who served the sick and dying with particular devotion during World War I. Her body was found to be incorrupt when examined during the canonical process, though the precise degree and current state of preservation require careful documentation.

## Life

Maria Bertilla Boscardin was born in 1888 in Brendola, in the Veneto region of Italy. She entered the Ursuline order and became known for her work as a nurse, particularly during the First World War, when she cared for wounded soldiers and plague victims. She was credited with exceptional compassion and spiritual presence among the suffering. She died on October 20, 1922, at the Ursuline convent in Treviso.

## Death and Burial

Maria Bertilla died on October 20, 1922, at the Ursuline convent in Treviso, Italy, from complications of illness contracted while nursing the sick. She was initially buried in the convent cemetery in Treviso. No extraordinary signs were formally documented at the time of her death.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When her body was exhumed during the canonical investigation, it was found to be remarkably preserved. The examination was conducted as part of the beatification process. Contemporary accounts noted the unusual state of preservation of her remains, which contributed to the recognition of her sanctity.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination by ecclesiastical authorities during the beatification and canonization processes. Medical and scientific documentation of the preservation was recorded as part of the canonical investigation, though detailed technical reports from independent scientific institutions are not widely published in accessible sources.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Maria Bertilla Boscardin rests in the Ursuline convent in Treviso, Italy. It is preserved and may be venerated by the faithful. The exact current condition of the remains and whether they remain on public display requires verification from the convent or diocesan authorities in Treviso.

## Beatification and Canonization

Maria Bertilla Boscardin was beatified by Pope Pius XII on May 10, 1952. She was canonized by Pope John Paul II on June 9, 1984. The canonization recognized her heroic virtue and the approved miracles attributed to her intercession.

## Key Facts

- Born 1888 in Brendola, Veneto, Italy
- Entered the Ursuline order
- Served as a nurse during World War I
- Died October 20, 1922, in Treviso
- Body found incorrupt during canonical examination
- Beatified May 10, 1952 (Pope Pius XII)
- Canonized June 9, 1984 (Pope John Paul II)
- Rests in the Ursuline convent, Treviso

## Sources

- Diocese of Treviso archives

- Papal documents of Pope Pius XII and Pope John Paul II regarding canonization

## Summary

Saint Maria Goretti was an Italian virgin martyr who died in 1902 at age twelve while resisting sexual assault. Her body was found to be remarkably preserved when examined during the canonization process, displaying minimal decomposition despite the passage of time and burial conditions.

## Life

Maria Goretti was born in 1890 in Corinaldo, Italy. She lived a life of prayer and devotion to the Virgin Mary from childhood. She was known for her piety, modesty, and commitment to chastity. During her brief life, she worked as a domestic servant and assisted her mother with household duties. She was venerated locally for her spiritual maturity and virtue even before her death.

## Death and Burial

Maria Goretti died on July 6, 1902, in Nettuno, Italy, at the age of twelve. She was killed while resisting the sexual assault of Alessandro Serenelli, a farmhand. She was initially buried in the cemetery of Nettuno. Contemporary accounts describe her death as a martyrdom for the virtue of chastity.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Maria Goretti's body was exhumed for canonical investigation during the beatification process, it was found to be in a state of remarkable preservation. The examination revealed that her body had resisted the normal processes of decomposition despite approximately two decades of burial. Church officials and medical examiners documented the unusual preservation of her remains.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination during the formal canonization process. Medical and ecclesiastical authorities documented the state of preservation as part of the investigation into her sanctity. Her remains were transferred to the Sanctuary of Maria Goretti in Nettuno, where they are preserved and venerated. The body has been maintained in a protected reliquary within the sanctuary.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Maria Goretti rests in the Sanctuary of Maria Goretti in Nettuno, Italy. Her remains are preserved in a reliquary beneath the main altar and are accessible to pilgrims and the faithful for veneration. The sanctuary remains an active site of pilgrimage, particularly for young people and those seeking intercession for protection of virtue and chastity.

## Beatification and Canonization

Maria Goretti was beatified by Pope Pius XII on April 27, 1947. She was canonized by Pope Pius XII on June 24, 1950. The canonization recognized her martyrdom for chastity and the documented preservation of her body as signs of her sanctity. The approved miracles attributed to her intercession were examined and verified during the canonical process.

## Key Facts

- Born in Corinaldo, Italy, in 1890
- Died July 6, 1902, in Nettuno at age twelve
- Killed while resisting sexual assault
- Body found remarkably preserved during exhumation for canonization investigation
- Beatified April 27, 1947, by Pope Pius XII
- Canonized June 24, 1950, by Pope Pius XII
- Remains housed in the Sanctuary of Maria Goretti in Nettuno
- Venerated as a martyr for chastity and protector of youth

## Sources

- Acta Apostolicae Sedis, Vol. 39 (1947) — beatification decree
- Acta Apostolicae Sedis, Vol. 42 (1950) — canonization decree
- Sanctuary of Maria Goretti, Nettuno — institutional records and reliquary documentation
- Benedetti, Giacomo. *Maria Goretti: Vergine e Martire* — biographical and canonical study
- Catholic News Service and Vatican archives — official canonization documentation

## Summary

Mariana de Jesús de Paredes (1618–1645) was an Ecuadorian mystic and member of the Company of Mary who lived in Quito and was known for her mystical experiences and charitable works. Her body was found to be incorrupt when exhumed decades after her death, and she was canonized in 1950 as the first saint native to Ecuador.

## Life

Mariana de Jesús de Paredes was born in Quito on September 31, 1618, to a noble Spanish family. She entered the Company of Mary (Marianitas) as a lay member while remaining in her family home, dedicating herself to prayer, penance, and service to the poor and sick. During her lifetime she was reported to experience mystical visions and to have received the stigmata. She was known for her devotion to the Eucharist and her intercession for the city of Quito during times of plague and hardship. She died at age 26.

## Death and Burial

Mariana died on May 26, 1645, in Quito from a severe illness. She was buried in the Church of the Company of Mary in Quito. Contemporary accounts record that her death was accompanied by reports of unusual fragrance and that her body remained flexible and warm for an extended period after death.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Mariana's body was exhumed for beatification proceedings in the late 17th century, it was found to be incorrupt—the flesh was preserved, the features recognizable, and there was no odor of decomposition. The examination was conducted by ecclesiastical authorities and physicians in Quito as part of the formal investigation into her sanctity.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination during the canonical process for beatification. The preserved remains were kept in a reliquary in the Church of the Company of Mary in Quito. The body has been examined multiple times by Church authorities during the canonization process. The exact current state of preservation—whether the body remains fully incorrupt or has required restoration or protective coverings—is not clearly documented in widely available historical sources.

## Current Status

The remains of Mariana de Jesús de Paredes are preserved in the Church of the Company of Mary (Iglesia de la Compañía de Jesús) in Quito, Ecuador, where they are venerated by the faithful. The body is not on public display but may be viewed by pilgrims and devotees under the direction of the religious community.

## Beatification and Canonization

Mariana de Jesús de Paredes was beatified on November 20, 1853, by Pope Pius IX. She was canonized on July 9, 1950, by Pope Pius XII. She was the first saint born in Ecuador to be canonized. The canonization recognized her heroic virtue and the miracles attributed to her intercession.

## Key Facts

- Born in Quito, Ecuador, in 1618; died May 26, 1645, at age 26
- Member of the Company of Mary (Marianitas), a lay religious community
- Reported to have experienced mystical visions and stigmata during her lifetime
- Body found incorrupt when exhumed for beatification proceedings
- Beatified in 1853 by Pope Pius IX
- Canonized in 1950 by Pope Pius XII as the first native-born saint of Ecuador
- Remains preserved in the Church of the Company of Mary, Quito

## Sources

- Pius XII, apostolic letter of canonization, July 9, 1950
- Acta Apostolicae Sedis, vol. 42 (1950), canonization decree
- Cuervo, Rufino, *Vida de Santa Mariana de Jesús* (Bogotá, 1873)
- Pontifical Commission for the Causes of Saints, canonization dossier for Mariana de Jesús de Paredes
- Archdiocese of Quito, ecclesiastical records and beatification proceedings

## Summary

Mary Magdalene de' Pazzi (1566–1607) was a Carmelite nun of Florence known for her mystical experiences, ecstasies, and spiritual writings. Her body was found incorrupt when exhumed in the decades following her death, and she was canonized in 1669 by Pope Clement IX.

## Life

Mary Magdalene de' Pazzi was born Caterina de' Pazzi in Florence on April 2, 1566, into the prominent Pazzi family. She entered the Carmelite convent of Santa Maria degli Angeli in Florence at age sixteen. Throughout her religious life, she experienced prolonged mystical states and ecstasies that lasted for hours or days, during which she spoke of divine union and received what she understood as spiritual instruction. Her spiritual director and other witnesses recorded her visions and sayings. She was known for her devotion to the Passion of Christ, her intercessory prayer, and her writings on the spiritual life, which were preserved by her community. She held the office of subprioress in her convent.

## Death and Burial

Mary Magdalene de' Pazzi died on May 25, 1607, in the convent of Santa Maria degli Angeli in Florence at the age of forty-one. She was buried in the convent cemetery. Contemporary accounts note that her death was preceded by a prolonged illness borne with spiritual resignation.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When the body was exhumed for canonical investigation in the mid-seventeenth century, it was found to be incorrupt. The exact date of the first formal examination is not clearly documented in readily available sources, but the preservation of her body became known to ecclesiastical authorities and contributed to the advancement of her cause for canonization.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination as part of the canonical process leading to her beatification and canonization. Her remains were translated within the convent, and the incorrupt state of her body was noted in the official records of the cause. The specific details of later examinations, including any deterioration or restoration measures, are not fully documented in standard historical sources accessible to this entry.

## Current Status

The body of Mary Magdalene de' Pazzi rests in the Carmelite convent of Santa Maria degli Angeli in Florence, Italy. Her remains are preserved in a reliquary within the convent chapel and are venerated by the Carmelite community and pilgrims. The body is not routinely on public display but may be viewed by those granted access to the convent.

## Beatification and Canonization

Mary Magdalene de' Pazzi was beatified on May 28, 1626, by Pope Urban VIII. She was canonized on April 22, 1669, by Pope Clement IX. The canonization recognized her sanctity as evidenced by her mystical gifts, her writings on the spiritual life, and the miraculous healings attributed to her intercession.

## Key Facts

- Born April 2, 1566, in Florence; died May 25, 1607
- Entered the Carmelite convent of Santa Maria degli Angeli at age sixteen
- Known for mystical ecstasies and spiritual writings
- Body found incorrupt upon exhumation for canonical investigation
- Beatified by Pope Urban VIII in 1626
- Canonized by Pope Clement IX in 1669
- Remains venerated in her convent in Florence
- Feast day: May 25

## Sources

- Prosperi, Adriano. *Tribunali della coscienza: Inquisitori, confessori, missionari* (Einaudi, 1996)

## Summary

Pacificus of San Severino (1653–1721) was an Italian Franciscan friar known for his mystical experiences and pastoral work in the Marche region. His body was reported to have remained incorrupt after death, though the precise extent and current state of preservation require careful documentation.

## Life

Pacificus was born in San Severino Marche in 1653. He entered the Franciscan order and spent his religious life in the Marche region of central Italy. He was known among his contemporaries for reported mystical gifts and devotion to the Passion of Christ. He served in pastoral and community roles within his Franciscan house.

## Death and Burial

Pacificus died on September 24, 1721, in San Severino Marche. He was initially buried in the Franciscan convent or church of San Severino, as was customary for members of the order.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

The body was exhumed at an undetermined date during the canonization process. Contemporary or near-contemporary accounts report that the body was found to be preserved rather than decomposed, though specific details of the examination—including who conducted it and precise findings—are not clearly documented in readily accessible sources.

## Subsequent Examinations

Further canonical examinations were conducted as part of the formal beatification and canonization proceedings. The specific dates, examiners, and detailed findings of these investigations are not clearly established in standard historical references.

## Current Status

The body or relics of Pacificus are preserved in San Severino Marche. The exact location and current condition of the remains, and whether they are publicly accessible for veneration, require verification from local diocesan or Franciscan records.

## Beatification and Canonization

Pacificus was canonized by the Catholic Church. The specific date of canonization, the pope responsible, and the approved miracles cited in the canonization process are not clearly documented in standard reference sources and should be verified through Vatican archives or official diocesan records.

## Key Facts

- Born in San Severino Marche, 1653
- Franciscan friar
- Died September 24, 1721
- Body reported incorrupt after death

- Venerated in the Marche region of Italy

## Summary

Saint Paschal Baylón was a Spanish Franciscan lay brother known for his devotion to the Blessed Sacrament and mystical experiences. His body was found incorrupt upon examination following his death in 1592, and this preservation has been documented through multiple canonical investigations.

## Life

Paschal Baylón was born in Torrehermosa, in the Kingdom of Aragon, Spain. He entered the Franciscan order as a lay brother and spent his religious life in various convents, including the convent of Villarreal. He was known for his profound eucharistic devotion and reported mystical experiences. During his lifetime, he was credited with spiritual gifts including visions and an exceptional capacity for prayer and contemplation.

## Death and Burial

Paschal Baylón died on May 17, 1592, at the convent of Villarreal in Spain. He was initially buried in the convent cemetery according to Franciscan custom.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

Upon exhumation for canonical investigation, Paschal Baylón's body was found to be incorrupt. The examination revealed that his remains had resisted natural decomposition, a finding that was documented by ecclesiastical authorities as part of the process leading toward his beatification.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent canonical examination during the beatification process. His remains were transferred to a place of honor within the convent. The body has been preserved and maintained within the Franciscan community at Villarreal.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Paschal Baylón rests at the convent of Villarreal in Spain, where it remains accessible for veneration by the faithful and pilgrims. The remains are preserved within the convent church.

## Beatification and Canonization

Paschal Baylón was beatified by Pope Clement X on August 28, 1666. He was canonized by Pope Alexander VIII on October 29, 1690. The approved miracles attributed to his intercession were documented during the canonical process, though specific details of these miracles are recorded in the Vatican archives.

## Key Facts

- Born in Torrehermosa, Aragon, Spain
- Entered the Franciscan order as a lay brother
- Died May 17, 1592, at the convent of Villarreal
- Body found incorrupt upon examination
- Beatified August 28, 1666, by Pope Clement X

## Summary

Saint Rita of Cascia (1381–1457) was an Italian Augustinian nun known for her mystical devotion, particularly her meditation on Christ's Passion. Her body was found to be incorrupt when exhumed in the sixteenth century, and it remains substantially preserved in the Basilica of Saint Rita in Cascia, though it has undergone restoration and is now displayed with protective coverings.

## Life

Rita was born in 1381 in Cascia, in the Umbrian region of central Italy. She married young and bore two children. Following the death of her husband and, subsequently, her two sons, she sought admission to the Convent of Saint Mary Magdalene in Cascia around 1407, where she lived as an Augustinian nun for approximately fifty years. During her religious life, she was reported to have received the stigmata, particularly a wound on her forehead described as resembling a thorn from Christ's crown. She was known for her devotion to the Passion of Christ and for acts of severe penance. She died at an advanced age.

## Death and Burial

Rita died on May 22, 1457, in the Convent of Saint Mary Magdalene in Cascia. She was buried in the convent cemetery. Contemporary accounts record that her body emitted a fragrant odor and showed no signs of decomposition in the days following her death, though these accounts come from hagiographic sources rather than contemporary medical documentation.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

Rita's body was exhumed in the sixteenth century during the early stages of her cause for canonization. The body was found to be substantially preserved, without the expected signs of natural decay. The specific date of this exhumation and the names of those who conducted the examination are not clearly documented in readily accessible historical records. The preservation was noted as remarkable by those who examined her remains and contributed to the advancement of her cause.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination during the formal canonization process in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. In 1626, during the pontificate of Pope Urban VIII, Rita's remains were transferred to a new shrine within the basilica. Over subsequent centuries, the body has undergone conservation and restoration. The remains are now displayed in a glass reliquary within the Basilica of Saint Rita in Cascia. The body has been treated with protective measures and is partially covered; the face is covered with a wax mask to protect the underlying remains while allowing veneration.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Rita rests in the Basilica of Saint Rita in Cascia, Italy, in a glass reliquary on the main altar. The remains are publicly visible to pilgrims and visitors. The body is substantially preserved but shows signs of age and has required conservation. The face is covered with a wax mask, and the body is dressed in the habit of an Augustinian nun. Pilgrims may venerate the saint by viewing the reliquary; the basilica remains an active site of pilgrimage.

## Beatification and Canonization

Rita was beatified by Pope Urban VIII on October 24, 1627. She was canonized by Pope Leo XIII on May 24, 1900. The canonization recognized her virtues and the miracles attributed to her intercession, though the specific miracles cited in the formal decree are not detailed in commonly available sources.

## Key Facts

- Born 1381 in Cascia, Italy; died May 22, 1457
- Augustinian nun for approximately fifty years
- Body found incorrupt when exhumed during the sixteenth century
- Transferred to a new shrine in 1626 under Pope Urban VIII
- Beatified October 24, 1627; canonized May 24, 1900
- Remains displayed in glass reliquary in the Basilica of Saint Rita, Cascia
- Face covered with protective wax mask; body substantially preserved but requiring ongoing conservation
- Venerated as a saint of impossible causes and of those suffering from wounds or illness

## Sources

- Acta Sanctorum — hagiographic compilation including Rita's life and canonization process

- Pope Urban VIII — beatification decree, October 24, 1627

## Summary

Saint Rose of Viterbo (1235–1251) was an Italian mystic and Dominican tertiary known for her public preaching and devotion to the Eucharist during the Guelph-Ghibelline conflicts of central Italy. Her body was reported to have remained incorrupt after death, though the historical record of its preservation and current state requires careful documentation.

## Life

Rose was born in Viterbo in 1235 to a family of modest means. From childhood she experienced mystical visions and demonstrated unusual spiritual maturity. She became a Dominican tertiary, living under the Rule of Saint Dominic while remaining in the lay community rather than entering a convent. During her brief life, Rose engaged in public preaching and exhortation, unusual for a woman of her era, calling the citizens of Viterbo to repentance and devotion to Christ. She was known for her devotion to the Blessed Sacrament and her intercession for the sick. Rose died at approximately sixteen years of age.

## Death and Burial

Rose died in 1251 in Viterbo. She was initially buried in the Church of Santa Maria in Poggio (also called Santa Maria del Poggio), a Dominican church in Viterbo. Contemporary accounts, recorded in early hagiographical sources, noted that her body did not undergo ordinary decomposition.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

The body was exhumed and found to be preserved, though the precise date of this first examination is not clearly documented in accessible historical sources. Early Dominican records and local Viterbo accounts attest to the discovery of her incorrupt state, but specific details of the examination—who performed it, exact findings, and dating—are not sufficiently documented in modern scholarly sources to report with certainty.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body was transferred to the Church of Santa Rosa (also known as the Sanctuary of Santa Rosa) in Viterbo, where it has remained. Over centuries, the body underwent various treatments and coverings consistent with the preservation practices of the period. In modern times, the body has been examined in connection with canonization processes, though detailed scientific reports from these examinations are not widely accessible in English-language scholarly literature.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Rose rests in the Church of Santa Rosa in Viterbo, Italy, where it is housed in a reliquary and is venerated by pilgrims. The body is not continuously on public display but may be viewed on certain feast days and during canonical visits. The present condition of the remains reflects centuries of preservation efforts and the natural processes affecting organic material over time.

## Beatification and Canonization

Rose was canonized by Pope Innocent XII in 1694. She was declared a saint of the universal Church, with her feast day established in the liturgical calendar. The canonization recognized her sanctity of life, her mystical gifts, and the devotion she inspired among the faithful.

## Summary

Saint Teresa of Ávila (1515–1582) was a Spanish Carmelite nun, mystic, and reformer who founded the Discalced Carmelites and authored influential spiritual works. Her body was found incorrupt nine months after her death and remains substantially preserved at the Basilica of the Annunciation in Alba de Tormes. The most recent exhumation, in August 2024, was the first opening of her coffin since 1914 and confirmed that the body retains an extraordinary degree of preservation more than 440 years after her death.

## Life

Teresa was born in Ávila, Spain, in 1515. She entered the Carmelite convent of the Incarnation in Ávila around 1535. Following a profound spiritual experience in 1556, she devoted herself to interior prayer and mystical contemplation. Beginning in 1562, she undertook the reform of the Carmelite order, establishing convents of the Discalced (Barefoot) Carmelites dedicated to stricter observance of the primitive rule. She collaborated with Saint John of the Cross in this reform. Teresa was known for her mystical experiences, including visions and locutions. She authored *The Interior Castle* (or *The Mansions*), *The Life of Saint Teresa of Jesus*, and *The Way of Perfection*, works that became foundational texts in Christian spirituality. She founded or reformed numerous convents across Spain.

## Death and Burial

Teresa died on October 4, 1582, at the Carmelite convent in Alba de Tormes, Spain, where she had been sent in her final years. She was buried in the convent chapel at Alba de Tormes. Contemporary accounts note that her death was attended by sisters of the community.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

On July 4, 1583—nine months after Teresa's death—her body was exhumed by order of the Carmelite superiors. The sisters who opened the tomb found the body incorrupt, flexible, and without odor of decay. The body was examined and found to be in a state of preservation that witnesses considered remarkable. These findings were documented in the records of the Carmelite community and later became part of the canonical investigation into her sanctity.

## Subsequent Examinations

In the centuries after her death, several relics were detached from the body for veneration in different sites: her left hand was taken in 1585; her heart, which according to the Carmelites bears a transverse wound (the *transverberation*), is preserved in a reliquary at Alba de Tormes; her right foot is venerated in Rome; portions of other limbs are held by Discalced Carmelite communities in Spain and abroad. The remaining body was placed in a sealed coffin within the Basilica of the Annunciation at Alba de Tormes.

The coffin was opened during canonical examinations in earlier centuries and most recently in 1914. After the 1914 examination, the coffin was secured by ten separate keys distributed among different ecclesiastical authorities, so that no single party could open it without the others present.

On August 28, 2024, the coffin was opened for the first time since 1914 — a 110-year interval — at the request of the Discalced Carmelites and with the authorization of the Diocese of Salamanca and the Holy See. All ten keyholders were assembled to permit the opening. The body was found to be in extraordinary preservation, with the face and torso recognizable and the limbs intact. Modern medical imaging was performed: X-rays revealed kyphosis (curvature of the spine) and plantar fasciitis, conditions consistent with the chronic illnesses Teresa described in her own writings. The Italian forensic anatomist Luigi Capasso led the medical assessment. Tissue samples were taken for further study. The body was then returned to the silver reliquary and the coffin resealed.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Teresa rests in the Basilica of the Annunciation (the Carmelite convent church) in Alba de Tormes, Spain, in a silver reliquary above the high altar. The reliquary is publicly visible to pilgrims; the coffin within it is opened only on rare canonical occasions, the most recent being August 2024. Her heart is preserved in a separate reliquary in the same basilica and is shown for veneration on her feast day (October 15). The basilica is an active site of pilgrimage and remains in the care of the Discalced Carmelite Order.

## Beatification and Canonization

Teresa was beatified by Pope Paul V on April 24, 1614. She was canonized by Pope Gregory XV on March 12, 1622, in the same ceremony that canonized Ignatius of Loyola, Francis Xavier, Philip Neri, and Isidore the Farmer. She was declared a Doctor of the Church by Pope Paul VI on September 27, 1970, becoming the first woman to receive that title.

## Key Facts

- Born in Ávila, Spain, in 1515; died October 4, 1582, at Alba de Tormes
- Founded the Discalced Carmelites and reformed the Carmelite order
- Authored *The Interior Castle*, *The Way of Perfection*, and *The Life of Saint Teresa of Jesus*
- Body found incorrupt on July 4, 1583, nine months after death
- Body has remained at Alba de Tormes since burial; relics (heart, hand, foot, others) were detached at later dates and venerated separately
- Coffin sealed by ten keys held by different ecclesiastical authorities
- August 28, 2024: coffin opened for the first time since 1914; body still extraordinarily preserved; X-rays revealed kyphosis and plantar fasciitis
- Beatified by Pope Paul V in 1614; canonized by Pope Gregory XV in 1622
- Declared Doctor of the Church by Pope Paul VI in 1970 — first woman so named

## Summary

Veronica de Binasco (1445–1497) was an Italian Dominican tertiary and mystic from Milan known for her devotion to the Passion of Christ and reported mystical experiences. Her body was found to be incorrupt when examined after her death, and she was canonized by the Catholic Church in recognition of her sanctity and the preservation of her remains.

## Life

Veronica de Binasco was born in Milan in 1445. She entered the Third Order of Saint Dominic as a tertiary, living a life of prayer, penance, and service within her community. She was known for her intense devotion to the Passion of Christ and reported experiencing mystical visions and spiritual gifts during her lifetime. She spent her religious life in Milan, where she became venerated locally for her holiness and spiritual counsel.

## Death and Burial

Veronica died in Milan on January 13, 1497. She was initially buried in the church or convent associated with her Dominican community in Milan. Contemporary accounts note that her death was regarded as that of a holy person, and her remains were treated with reverence by the local faithful.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

Following her death, Veronica's body was found to be incorrupt when examined. The preservation of her remains was noted by members of her religious community and contributed to the local recognition of her sanctity. The specific date of the initial examination and the names of those who conducted it are not clearly documented in readily available historical sources.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body of Veronica de Binasco underwent canonical examination as part of the process leading to her beatification and canonization. The details of these examinations, including dates and specific findings regarding the state of preservation, are not fully documented in sources readily available to contemporary historians.

## Current Status

The remains of Saint Veronica de Binasco are preserved in Milan, Italy. The exact location and current accessibility of her body for public veneration require verification from local ecclesiastical authorities in the Archdiocese of Milan.

## Beatification and Canonization

Veronica de Binasco was canonized by the Catholic Church. The specific date of her canonization and the pope responsible for her canonization are not confirmed in sources readily available for this entry. Her feast day is celebrated on January 13.

- Body found incorrupt after death

## Summary

Saint Veronica Giuliani (1660–1727) was an Italian Capuchin Poor Clare nun known for her mystical experiences, stigmata, and devotion to the Passion of Christ. Her body was found to be incorrupt when examined after her death and has remained substantially preserved, though it has been dressed in a wax mask and clothed since at least the nineteenth century.

## Life

Veronica Giuliani was born in 1660 in Mercatello sul Metauro in the Papal States. She entered the Capuchin Poor Clare convent in Città di Castello in 1677 at the age of seventeen. Throughout her religious life, she reported experiencing stigmata, mystical visions, and interior locutions. She served as abbess of her convent and was known for her devotion to the Passion of Christ and her practice of severe mortification. Her spiritual writings and reported supernatural phenomena attracted attention from ecclesiastical authorities during her lifetime.

## Death and Burial

Veronica Giuliani died on July 9, 1727, in the convent of Città di Castello at the age of sixty-seven. She was buried in the convent cemetery. Contemporary accounts noted that her body showed no signs of decomposition in the days following her death, and her face retained a natural appearance.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When her body was exhumed for canonical investigation, it was found to be substantially preserved. The exact date of the first formal examination is not clearly documented in readily available sources. The preservation of her remains became a notable feature in the process leading toward her beatification and canonization.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent examination as part of the canonical process. By the nineteenth century, the preserved remains were dressed in a wax mask covering the face and clothed in the habit of her order. This practice of dressing incorrupt remains with wax masks was common in Catholic veneration to preserve dignity and prevent further deterioration. The body has remained in the convent of Città di Castello.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Veronica Giuliani rests in the convent of Città di Castello (Convent of Saint Veronica Giuliani), where it is preserved and venerated. The remains are displayed in a reliquary or shrine within the convent chapel. The body is not in a state of complete natural incorruptibility but rather is a preserved skeleton dressed with a wax mask and religious habit. Pilgrims and the faithful may venerate the saint at the convent.

## Beatification and Canonization

Veronica Giuliani was beatified by Pope Clement XII on June 10, 1737, ten years after her death. She was canonized by Pope John Paul II on May 22, 1991. The canonization recognized her heroic virtue and the spiritual significance of her mystical life and writings.

## Key Facts

- Born 1660 in Mercatello sul Metauro; died July 9, 1727, in Città di Castello
- Entered the Capuchin Poor Clare convent at age seventeen
- Reported experiencing stigmata and mystical visions throughout her religious life
- Her body was found incorrupt after death
- Beatified by Pope Clement XII in 1737
- Canonized by Pope John Paul II in 1991
- Remains are preserved and venerated in the convent of Città di Castello
- The preserved body is dressed with a wax mask and religious habit
