## Summary

Vincent de Paul (1581–1660) was a French priest and founder of the Congregation of the Mission and the Daughters of Charity, dedicated to serving the poor and enslaved. His body was reported to be incorrupt at early examinations following his death in Paris, though the present state of his remains reflects the typical preservation methods applied to canonized saints of his era.

## Life

Vincent de Paul was born in Pouy, in the Landes region of southwestern France. He was ordained a priest and initially served as a chaplain and tutor. Following a spiritual conversion, he devoted himself to the service of the poor, the sick, and the enslaved. In 1625, he founded the Congregation of the Mission (Vincentians) to evangelize rural poor populations. In 1633, he co-founded with Saint Louise de Marillac the Daughters of Charity, a community of women religious dedicated to nursing, teaching, and social welfare. Vincent established hospitals, orphanages, and relief programs during the civil upheavals of the Fronde. He was known for his practical charity, organizational ability, and spiritual direction of clergy and laity.

## Death and Burial

Vincent de Paul died on September 27, 1660, in Paris at the motherhouse of the Congregation of the Mission on the Rue Saint-Denis. He was initially buried in the chapel of the Vincentian house in Paris. Contemporary accounts record that his death was attended by members of his communities and that his funeral was marked by widespread mourning among the poor he had served.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Vincent's body was exhumed for examination during the early stages of his canonization process, it was reported to be in a state of preservation. The exact date of this first examination is not consistently documented in accessible sources. Early hagiographical accounts and Vincentian records note that his body showed resistance to decay, though specific details of the examination—who conducted it, precise findings, and dating—are not uniformly recorded in modern scholarly literature.

## Subsequent Examinations

Vincent's body was translated and examined multiple times during the canonical process leading to his beatification and canonization. Like many canonized saints of the seventeenth century, his remains were eventually placed in a reliquary and dressed according to the liturgical practices of the time. The body is no longer displayed in its original form; instead, a wax effigy or mask was created to represent the saint for public veneration, a common practice for important relics in the Catholic Church.

## Current Status

The relics of Saint Vincent de Paul are preserved in the chapel of the Vincentian motherhouse (now the Saint Vincent de Paul Chapel) in Paris, located at 95 Rue de Sèvres in the 6th arrondissement. The saint is represented by a recumbent figure dressed in his priestly vestments, with the actual relics secured beneath. The chapel is open to the public and remains a site of pilgrimage and veneration. The body itself is not exposed to view; rather, the reliquary and wax representation serve as the focus of devotion.

## Beatification and Canonization

Vincent de Paul was beatified by Pope Clement XII on August 13, 1729. He was canonized by Pope Clement XII on June 16, 1737. The canonization was based on the recognition of his sanctity, his foundational works in charity and religious life, and miracles attributed to his intercession, though the specific miracles approved are not detailed here without access to the official papal documents.

## Key Facts

- Born 1581 in Pouy, France; died September 27, 1660, in Paris
- Founded the Congregation of the Mission (Vincentians) in 1625
- Co-founded the Daughters of Charity with Saint Louise de Marillac in 1633
- Dedicated his life to serving the poor, sick, enslaved, and abandoned
- Beatified August 13, 1729, by Pope Clement XII
- Canonized June 16, 1737, by Pope Clement XII
- His relics are preserved in Paris at the Vincentian motherhouse chapel
- Early reports indicated his body resisted decay; now represented by a wax effigy over relics
- Patron saint of charitable workers and the poor

## Summary

Vincent Ferrer (1350–1419) was a Spanish Dominican friar, theologian, and missionary renowned for his preaching campaigns across Western Europe and his reported supernatural gifts. His body was reported incorrupt when examined after his death in Vannes, France, though the present state of preservation and any subsequent deterioration require careful documentation.

## Life

Vincent was born in Valencia, Spain, in 1350 to a noble family. He entered the Dominican Order and studied theology, becoming a master of theology and a prominent preacher. During his lifetime, he was credited with extraordinary preaching abilities and reported miracles of healing and conversion. He traveled extensively throughout France, Spain, Italy, and other regions, drawing large crowds and reportedly converting thousands. He was known for his austere lifestyle and his emphasis on penance and moral reform.

## Death and Burial

Vincent died on April 5, 1419, in Vannes, Brittany, France, where he had been conducting missionary work. He was initially buried in the Dominican convent chapel in Vannes. Contemporary accounts record that his death was marked by signs of sanctity among the local faithful.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Vincent's body was examined following his death, it was reported to be incorrupt—that is, showing no signs of natural decomposition. The examination was conducted by Dominican friars and local ecclesiastical authorities in Vannes. However, specific details of this initial examination—the exact date, the names of examiners, and precise descriptions of the body's condition—are not consistently documented in readily accessible historical sources.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body of Vincent Ferrer underwent examination during the canonization process. His remains were translated and venerated within the Dominican community. However, detailed records of subsequent medical or scientific examinations, any deterioration of the body, or the application of preservative measures such as wax masks are not well-documented in standard historical sources available for this entry.

## Current Status

Vincent Ferrer's relics are housed in the Basilica of Saint Vincent Ferrer in Vannes, France. The present physical state of his remains—whether fully incorrupt, partially preserved, or represented by relics in a reliquary—requires verification from current institutional sources at the basilica. The shrine remains a site of pilgrimage and veneration.

## Beatification and Canonization

Vincent Ferrer was beatified by Pope Pius II in 1455. He was canonized by Pope Sixtus V on June 29, 1588. The canonization recognized his sanctity and the miracles attributed to his intercession, though specific miracle accounts are not detailed here without primary source verification.

## Summary

Vincent Pallotti (1795–1850) was an Italian priest, missionary, and founder of the Pallottine Society, dedicated to apostolic work and the sanctification of the laity. His body was found to be incorrupt when exhumed in the nineteenth century, though the precise condition and any subsequent preservation methods require careful documentation.

## Life

Vincent Pallotti was born in Rome on April 21, 1795. He was ordained a priest and became known for his pastoral work, spiritual direction, and missionary zeal. In 1835, he founded the Society of the Catholic Apostolate (Pallottine Society), an organization dedicated to promoting active participation of lay faithful in the Church's apostolic mission. He was recognized during his lifetime for his devotion to the Eucharist, his work with the poor, and his efforts to foster missionary vocations. He spent most of his priestly ministry in Rome.

## Death and Burial

Vincent Pallotti died in Rome on January 22, 1850. He was initially buried in the church of the Society of the Catholic Apostolate in Rome. Contemporary accounts noted the veneration that followed his death among those who knew of his sanctity and apostolic labors.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

When Pallotti's body was exhumed for canonical processes related to his beatification cause, it was found to be incorrupt. The examination confirmed the preservation of his remains, though detailed records of the specific examination date and examining officials require verification from canonical archives.

## Subsequent Examinations

Following the initial discovery of incorruptibility, Pallotti's body underwent examination as part of the formal beatification and canonization processes. His remains were transferred and enshrined in the motherhouse of the Pallottine Society in Rome. The specific details of any later examinations, restoration work, or use of protective coverings are not reliably documented in readily accessible sources.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Vincent Pallotti rests in the Church of the Holy Apostles (Chiesa dei Santi Apostoli) or the motherhouse of the Pallottine Society in Rome. The remains are venerated by members of his religious community and pilgrims. Access and visibility depend on the policies of the Pallottine Society.

## Beatification and Canonization

Vincent Pallotti was beatified by Pope Pius IX on December 16, 1963, and canonized by Pope Paul VI on January 20, 1963. The canonization recognized his exemplary apostolic life and the miracles attributed to his intercession.

## Summary

Saint Zita of Lucca (1218–1272) was an Italian domestic servant and mystic venerated for her charity, humility, and reported miracles during her lifetime. Her body was found incorrupt when exhumed in the early sixteenth century and has remained substantially preserved, though it has been dressed in a wax mask and ecclesiastical vestments for centuries.

## Life

Zita was born in 1218 in Monsagrati, near Lucca, Italy. At age twelve she entered domestic service in the household of the Fatinelli family in Lucca, where she remained for nearly fifty years. She became known for her austere personal practices—including fasting, prayer, and voluntary mortification—and for her extraordinary charity toward the poor and sick, often distributing her employers' provisions to those in need. Contemporaries attributed to her gifts of prophecy, miraculous healings, and supernatural knowledge of hidden sins. She was deeply devoted to the Eucharist and maintained a life of contemplative prayer amid her daily labors.

## Death and Burial

Zita died on 27 April 1272 in Lucca at approximately fifty-four years of age. She was buried in the Church of San Frediano in Lucca. Contemporary accounts record that her body emitted a sweet fragrance and that devotion to her began immediately after her death, with the faithful seeking her intercession for healings and spiritual aid.

## Discovery of Incorruptibility

In 1580, more than three centuries after her death, the body of Saint Zita was exhumed during renovation work at the Church of San Frediano. The body was found to be substantially incorrupt—the flesh preserved, the facial features recognizable, and the limbs intact. The discovery prompted ecclesiastical investigation and contributed to the formal process of her canonization. Contemporary witnesses recorded their astonishment at the preservation of the remains.

## Subsequent Examinations

The body underwent canonical examination in connection with her beatification process in the sixteenth century. In 1696, following her canonization, the body was formally recognized and transferred to a shrine within the Church of San Frediano. Over subsequent centuries, as natural deterioration occurred and the remains became more fragile, the body was dressed in ecclesiastical vestments and a wax mask was applied to the face to preserve the saint's appearance for veneration. The body has remained in this state—substantially skeletal beneath the wax mask and vestments—into the modern period.

## Current Status

The body of Saint Zita rests in a glass reliquary shrine in the Church of San Frediano in Lucca, Italy. The remains are publicly visible to pilgrims and the faithful. The body is displayed dressed in religious vestments with a wax mask covering the skull, presenting the saint in a state of formal ecclesiastical repose. Veneration occurs through prayer before the shrine and the traditional invocation of her intercession.

## Beatification and Canonization

Saint Zita was beatified by Pope Sixtus IV in 1480. She was canonized by Pope Gregory XV on 20 April 1622. The canonization recognized her virtues of charity, obedience, and sanctity, as well as miracles attributed to her intercession. The specific miracles cited in the canonical process are not detailed in readily accessible modern sources.

## Key Facts

- Born 1218 in Monsagrati, near Lucca; died 27 April 1272
- Spent nearly fifty years in domestic service in the Fatinelli household
- Body exhumed in 1580 and found substantially incorrupt
- Beatified by Pope Sixtus IV in 1480
- Canonized by Pope Gregory XV in 1622
- Remains housed in a glass reliquary in the Church of San Frediano, Lucca
- Body preserved beneath wax mask and ecclesiastical vestments
- Venerated as patroness of domestic workers and servants
